arXiv:2604.14835v1 [math-ph] 16 Apr 2026

Hamiltonian Monodromy in a Tavis-Cummings System
with an A2A_{2} Singularity

Konstantinos Efstathiou k.efstathiou@dukekunshan.edu.cn Division of Natural and Applied Sciences and Zu Chongzhi Center, Duke Kunshan University, 8 Duke Avenue, 215316 Kunshan, Jiangsu, China Gabriela Jocelyn Gutierrez-Guillen gabriela.gutierrez@ens-lyon.fr Unité de Mathématiques Pures et Appliquées UMR 5669 CNRS, École Normale Supérieure de Lyon Site Monod, 46 Allée d’Italie, 69364 Lyon Cedex 07, France Pavao Mardešić pavao.mardesic@ube.fr Institut de Mathématiques de Bourgogne UMR 5584 CNRS, Université Bourgogne Europe, 9 avenue Alain Savary, BP 47870, 21078 Dijon Cedex, France Department of Mathematics, Faculty of Science, University of Zagreb, Bijenička 30, 10 000 Zagreb, Croatia Laboratorio Internacional Solomon Lefschetz IRL 2001 CNRS-UNAM, Instituto de Matemáticas Unidad Cuernavaca, Mexico Dominique Sugny dominique.sugny@u-bourgogne.fr Université Bourgogne Europe, CNRS, Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire Carnot de Bourgogne ICB UMR 6303, 21000 Dijon, France
(April 16, 2026)
Abstract

Singular Lagrangian fibrations arising from three-degree-of-freedom integrable Hamiltonian systems remain largely unexplored. While several results describe the global structure of large classes of systems with two degrees of freedom, only a few examples are understood in higher dimensions. We present a three-degree-of-freedom system derived from the two-spin Tavis-Cummings model whose singular Lagrangian fibration exhibits a topology that has not been observed in other physical models. We show that the most degenerate singular fiber is homeomorphic to 𝐒2×𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{1} with a singularity of A2A_{2} type. We further describe the bifurcation diagram and the global topology of the fibration, and we compute its Hamiltonian monodromy.

MSC2020: 37J35, 53D20, 70H06, 70H33

Keywords—Integrable Hamiltonian systems, singular Lagrangian fibrations, Hamiltonian monodromy, Tavis-Cummings system

1 Introduction

The Tavis-Cummings (TC) system is a fundamental model in quantum optics of the interaction between matter and electromagnetic fields  [34]. The model describes the coupling between NN two-level atoms and a single-mode quantized radiation field within a lossless cavity [24]. It is itself a generalization of the Jaynes-Cummings (JC) system which applies to the case of a single atom, N=1N=1 [27]. The JC and TC systems have been extensively studied from classical and quantum points of view. Notably, from the quantum point of view, the TC system has been realized experimentally in a superconducting circuit for quantum information processing [30, 19].

In this work we consider the classical analogue of the quantum TC system, where the two-level quantum atoms are replaced by classical spins and the quantized cavity mode is replaced by a harmonic oscillator. The classical TC system with NN spins is an (N+1)(N+1)-degree-of-freedom Liouville integrable Hamiltonian system which, moreover, admits a Lax pair representation for any NN [29, 7]. The Lax pair considerably simplifies the analysis of the TC system, and leads to explicit solutions of the equations of motion in terms of hyperelliptic functions [38]. Despite this explicit description of the TC dynamics, the qualitative understanding of the global topology of the system with N≥2N\geq 2 spins is still limited.

The JC system, corresponding to N=1N=1, is well understood. This is a two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) integrable Hamiltonian system with a global circle action, and it has a focus-focus singularity for a range of parameters. The existence of a focus-focus singularity in a 2-DOF Hamiltonian system implies the non-triviality of Hamiltonian monodromy [14, 37, 40], and we note that the singular Lagrangian fibration near a focus-focus singularity is locally isomorphic to the A1A_{1} singularity [8, 2]. Because of the focus-focus singularity, the JC system serves as one of the main examples in the study of semitoric systems and their symplectic classification [31], and a complete description of its semitoric invariants is known [32, 1]. The spectral Lax pair approach has also been used for the analysis of the global topology of the JC system [5], and to determine its Hamiltonian monodromy [6, 23]. Much less is known about the topology of the singular Lagrangian fibration of the TC system with N≥2N\geq 2.

This contrast between 2-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems and systems with three or more degrees of freedom is not specific to the TC system. The topology of the singular Lagrangian fibrations of 2-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems is well understood [10], and there is an extensive collection of physical examples with two degrees of freedom manifesting different aspects of the mathematical theory, see [3, 11, 16]. However, very few studies have considered systems with three or more degrees of freedom and most of these have focused on 3-DOF systems with a global 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action, where the analysis is facilitated by symplectic reduction to 1-DOF. Examples include the Lagrange top [3, 11, 12], the resonant swing spring [15, 21], and the axially symmetric 1:1:−21{:}1{:}{-}2 resonance [17].

Coming back to the TC system, and—in particular—the 3-DOF system with N=2N=2 spins, we note that it possesses a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action but lacks a global 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action, and thus it cannot be symplectically reduced to 1-DOF. For this system, the spectral Lax pair approach has been successfully used to parameterize its singularities and determine their stability type [5]. Nevertheless, the implications of these findings for the global topology of the two-spin TC system have not been further elaborated.

A natural question is whether among the qualitatively different types of singular Lagrangian fibrations that can appear in the TC system for different parameter choices, there are any novel cases that have not previously appeared in the literature. A careful analysis of the spectral curve of the TC system, described in detail in Appendix D, allowed us to identify a choice of parameters for which the curve has a high-degree of degeneracy possessing two complex conjugate triple roots. We call the two-spin TC system with this choice of parameters, the special Tavis-Cummings (STC) system, see Definition 2.

The STC system exhibits an intriguing global topology that has not been previously found in other physical examples. We analyze in detail the singularities of the STC system and its Hamiltonian monodromy, and we show that:

  1. (i)

    The STC system has four threads of rank-11 focus-focus-regular singularities that meet at a degenerate critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}, which we call the central critical value, see Section 4 and Figure 1.

  2. (ii)

    The singular fiber of the integral map corresponding to the degenerate critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} is topologically equivalent to 𝐒2×𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{1} and contains an 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} orbit of degenerate singularities. We call the corresponding degenerate singularity c∗c_{*} in the reduced space, the central singularity.

  3. (iii)

    The 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} reduced fibration near the central singularity c∗c_{*} is locally isomorphic to the fibration of f:𝐂2×𝐑→𝐂×𝐑f:\mathbf{C}^{2}\times\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{C}\times\mathbf{R}, f​(x,y,κ)=(y2+x3+κ​x,κ)f(x,y,\kappa)=(y^{2}+x^{3}+\kappa x,\kappa), near 0, see Theorem 15. That is, the degenerate singularity in the reduced space is locally isomorphic to the A2A_{2} singularity [2].

  4. (iv)

    The fundamental group of the set of regular values of the integral map is isomorphic to the free group of three generators 𝐅3=𝐙∗𝐙∗𝐙\mathbf{F}_{3}=\mathbf{Z}\ast\mathbf{Z}\ast\mathbf{Z}. For the choice of generators γ1\gamma_{1}, γ2\gamma_{2}, γ3\gamma_{3} described in Section 5.2 the corresponding monodromy matrices are

    M​(γ1)=[100011001],M​(γ2)=[1000100−11],M​(γ3)=[1000210−10].M(\gamma_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&1\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix},\quad M(\gamma_{2})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&-1&1\end{bmatrix},\quad M(\gamma_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&2&1\\ 0&-1&0\end{bmatrix}.
  5. (v)

    For the value of the momentum generating the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action corresponding to the central singularity c∗c_{*}, the symplectic 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} reduction of the STC system produces a 2-DOF integrable Hamiltonian system with an isolated critical value but no global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action, see Figure 1 with K=k∗K=k^{*}. The singular fiber corresponding to the isolated critical value is homeomorphic to 𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2} and contains an A2A_{2} singularity. The Hamiltonian monodromy around the isolated critical value is

    [11−10].\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\ -1&0\end{bmatrix}.

The STC system presents a novel and interesting physical example of a 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian system with non-trivial Hamiltonian monodromy and the appearance of an A2A_{2} singularity. From this point of view, it is an important addition to our short list of well understood singular Lagrangian fibrations of 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems. Such examples will play an important role in any future attempts toward the topological and symplectic classification of 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems. Moreover, such examples are useful in the context of mirror symmetry [22, 9].

The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 we present the Hamiltonian and the integral map of the classical two-spin TC system and the special two-spin TC (STC) system. In Section 3 we discuss the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action of the TC system, its fixed points, and local symplectic reduction. In Section 4 we parameterize and classify the singularities of the TC system, obtaining Figure 1. We note that even though we do not use the spectral Lax pair approach, we recover the results of [5] for rank-0 and rank-11 singularities, while slightly improving on the previous results for rank-22 singularities which do not provide explicit parameter ranges. In Section 5, we numerically calculate the Hamiltonian monodromy of the STC system. In Section 6 we show that the STC system in the vicinity of the central singularity has a local normal form corresponding to an A2A_{2} singularity, that is the fibration of the STC system is locally topologically equivalent to the fibration given by the versal unfolding of the A2A_{2} singularity. We then compute the Picard-Lefschetz monodromy of the normal form and we show its correspondence with the Hamiltonian monodromy of the system. We give conclusions and prospective views in Section 7. Finally, in the Appendices, we provide additional information and details on the reduction of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action, the proof of the parameterization of rank-22 singularities, the numerical computation of Hamiltonian monodromy, and a brief description of the spectral Lax pair formalism.

2 The classical two-spin Tavis-Cummings system

The classical two-spin Tavis-Cummings system is a 3-DOF Hamiltonian system defined on the symplectic manifold M=𝐒u2×𝐒v2×𝐑2M=\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\times\mathbf{R}^{2}. The symplectic form on each of the spheres

𝐒u2={(u1,u2,u3):u12+u22+u32=1},𝐒v2={(v1,v2,v3):v12+v22+v32=1},\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}=\{(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3}):u_{1}^{2}+u_{2}^{2}+u_{3}^{2}=1\},\ \mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}=\{(v_{1},v_{2},v_{3}):v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}+v_{3}^{2}=1\},

is the standard area form, while 𝐑2\mathbf{R}^{2} has canonical coordinates (q,p)(q,p). The corresponding Poisson algebra is isomorphic to so⁡(3)⊕so⁡(3)⊕sp⁡(2,𝐑)\operatorname{so}(3)\oplus\operatorname{so}(3)\oplus\operatorname{sp}(2,\mathbf{R}), where the non-vanishing Poisson brackets are given by

{ui,uj}=∑kεi​j​k​uk,{vi,vj}=∑kεi​j​k​vk,{q,p}=−{p,q}=1.\displaystyle\{u_{i},u_{j}\}=\sum_{k}\varepsilon_{ijk}u_{k},\ \{v_{i},v_{j}\}=\sum_{k}\varepsilon_{ijk}v_{k},\ \{q,p\}=-\{p,q\}=1.

The Hamiltonian function of the two-spin TC system is

H=δ1​u3+δ2​v3+ω2​(p2+q2)+2​g​(q​u1−p​u2)+2​g​(q​v1−p​v2),\displaystyle H=\delta_{1}u_{3}+\delta_{2}v_{3}+\frac{\omega}{2}(p^{2}+q^{2})+\sqrt{2}g(qu_{1}-pu_{2})+\sqrt{2}g(qv_{1}-pv_{2}),

where δ1\delta_{1}, δ2\delta_{2}, ω\omega, gg are real parameters.

We introduce the complex coordinates

u=u1+i​u2,v=v1+i​v2,z=p+i​q,\displaystyle u=u_{1}+iu_{2},\ v=v_{1}+iv_{2},\ z=p+iq,

with Poisson brackets

{u,u3}=i​u,{u¯,u3}=−i​u¯,{u,u¯}=−2​i​u3,\displaystyle\{u,u_{3}\}=iu,\ \{\bar{u},u_{3}\}=-i\bar{u},\ \{u,\bar{u}\}=-2iu_{3},
{v,v3}=i​v,{v¯,v3}=−i​v¯,{v,v¯}=−2​i​v3,{z,z¯}=2​i.\displaystyle\{v,v_{3}\}=iv,\ \{\bar{v},v_{3}\}=-i\bar{v},\ \{v,\bar{v}\}=-2iv_{3},\{z,\bar{z}\}=2i.

Using these coordinates, HH is written as

H=δ1​u3+δ2​v3+ω2​z​z¯+i2​g​(u​z¯−u¯​z)+i2​g​(v​z¯−v¯​z),\displaystyle H=\delta_{1}u_{3}+\delta_{2}v_{3}+\frac{\omega}{2}z\bar{z}+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}g(u\bar{z}-\bar{u}z)+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}g(v\bar{z}-\bar{v}z),

The function HH is invariant under the Hamiltonian 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action

φK:𝐒1×M→M:(ei​t,(u,u3;v,v3;z))↦(ei​t​u,u3;ei​t​v,v3;ei​t​z),\displaystyle\varphi_{K}:\mathbf{S}^{1}\times M\to M:(e^{it},(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z))\mapsto(e^{it}u,u_{3};e^{it}v,v_{3};e^{it}z), (1)

generated by the Hamiltonian flow of the momentum

K=u3+v3+12​z​z¯,K=u_{3}+v_{3}+\frac{1}{2}z\bar{z}, (2)

with Hamiltonian vector field XKX_{K} satisfying

XK​(u)=i​u,XK​(u3)=0,XK​(v)=i​v,XK​(v3)=0,XK​(z)=i​z.X_{K}(u)=iu,\ X_{K}(u_{3})=0,\ X_{K}(v)=iv,\ X_{K}(v_{3})=0,\ X_{K}(z)=iz. (3)

Although there is only one obvious symmetry in the system, it is well known that the Hamiltonian HH is Liouville integrable [5]. In addition to the momentum KK, two other integrals are given by

H1\displaystyle H_{1} =(δ1−ω)​u3+i2​g​(u​z¯−u¯​z)−g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯+u¯​v+2​u3​v3),\displaystyle=(\delta_{1}-\omega)u_{3}+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}g(u\bar{z}-\bar{u}z)-\frac{g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v+2u_{3}v_{3}), (4a)
H2\displaystyle H_{2} =(δ2−ω)​v3+i2​g​(v​z¯−v¯​z)+g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯+u¯​v+2​u3​v3).\displaystyle=(\delta_{2}-\omega)v_{3}+\frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}g(v\bar{z}-\bar{v}z)+\frac{g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v+2u_{3}v_{3}). (4b)

Then,

H=H1+H2+ω​K.H=H_{1}+H_{2}+\omega K.

Both H1H_{1} and H2H_{2} are invariant under the Hamiltonian 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action (1), which implies {H1,K}={H2,K}=0\{H_{1},K\}=\{H_{2},K\}=0. Moreover, a direct computation shows that {H1,H2}=0\{H_{1},H_{2}\}=0. Therefore, the map

F=(H1,H2,K):M→𝐑3,F=(H_{1},H_{2},K):M\to\mathbf{R}^{3}, (5)

defines a Liouville integrable system.

Remark 1.

In the case δ1=δ2\delta_{1}=\delta_{2}, the Hamiltonian dynamics is completely different. In this case, there are three independent integrals HH, KK, and J=∑k=13(uk+vk)2J=\sum_{k=1}^{3}(u_{k}+v_{k})^{2}. The momentum KK generates a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action, while JJ generates a periodic flow and thus a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action up to time rescaling.

The following expressions for the Hamiltonian vector fields XH1X_{H_{1}}, XH2X_{H_{2}} will be used in Section 4 for deriving the singularities of FF:

XH1​(u)=(δ1−ω)​i​u−2​g​u3​z+2​i​g2δ2−δ1​(u3​v−v3​u),\displaystyle X_{H_{1}}(u)=(\delta_{1}-\omega)iu-\sqrt{2}gu_{3}z+\frac{2ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u_{3}v-v_{3}u), XH1​(v)=−2​i​g2δ2−δ1​(u3​v−v3​u),\displaystyle X_{H_{1}}(v)=-\frac{2ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u_{3}v-v_{3}u), (6)
XH1​(u3)=g2​(u​z¯+u¯​z)+i​g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯−u¯​v),\displaystyle X_{H_{1}}(u_{3})=\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}(u\bar{z}+\bar{u}z)+\frac{ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v), XH1​(v3)=−i​g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯−u¯​v),\displaystyle X_{H_{1}}(v_{3})=-\frac{ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v),
XH1​(z)=−2​g​u,\displaystyle X_{H_{1}}(z)=-\sqrt{2}gu,

and

XH2​(u)=−2​i​g2δ2−δ1​(u3​v−u​v3),\displaystyle X_{H_{2}}(u)=-\frac{2ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u_{3}v-uv_{3}), XH2​(v)=(δ2−ω)​i​v−2​g​v3​z+2​i​g2δ2−δ1​(u3​v−u​v3),\displaystyle X_{H_{2}}(v)=(\delta_{2}-\omega)iv-\sqrt{2}gv_{3}z+\frac{2ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u_{3}v-uv_{3}), (7)
XH2​(u3)=−i​g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯−u¯​v),\displaystyle X_{H_{2}}(u_{3})=-\frac{ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v), XH2​(v3)=g2​(v​z¯+v¯​z)+i​g2δ2−δ1​(u​v¯−u¯​v),\displaystyle X_{H_{2}}(v_{3})=\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}(v\bar{z}+\bar{v}z)+\frac{ig^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v),
XH2​(z)=−2​g​v.\displaystyle X_{H_{2}}(z)=-\sqrt{2}gv.

As noted in the Introduction, in this work we focus on a special case of the two-spin Tavis–Cummings system. The corresponding parameter values were obtained using a spectral Lax pair approach [26], where the problem of finding singularities is translated into the problem of finding multiple roots of a polynomial, whose vanishing locus is called the spectral curve [5]. For the two-spin TC system, by searching for parameters such that this degree 66 polynomial exhibits two complex conjugate triple roots, we find that it is necessary to analyze the parameter values satisfying the resonance condition δ1+δ2=2​ω\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}=2\omega, with δ1≠δ2\delta_{1}\neq\delta_{2}, as detailed in Appendix D. For computational simplicity, we further restrict attention to the following specific values of the TC parameters.

Definition 2.

The special Tavis-Cummings (STC) system is the two-spin Tavis-Cummings system described by the integral map F=(H1,H2,K)F=(H_{1},H_{2},K) with H1H_{1}, H2H_{2} in Eq. (4) and parameter values

δ1=12,δ2=32,ω=1,g=1.\delta_{1}=\frac{1}{2},\quad\delta_{2}=\frac{3}{2},\quad\omega=1,\quad g=1. (8)

Note that several of the results in Section 3 and Section 4 apply to the general two-spin TC system, and we explicitly mention when a result applies only to the STC system. Results in Section 5 and Section 6 apply specifically to the STC system.

3 The 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} symmetry of the Tavis-Cummings system

In this section, we consider the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K} (1) of the Tavis-Cummings system, we determine the fixed points of the action, and we describe how to locally reduce the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action. We refer the interested reader to Appendix A for a discussion of the global reduction of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action using algebraic invariants, and a description of the topology of the reduced spaces K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}.

Proposition 3.

The 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K} (1) on M=𝐒u2×𝐒v2×𝐂M=\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\times\mathbf{C} is free outside the fixed points fpσu,σv=(0,σu;0,σv;0)\mathrm{fp}_{\sigma_{u},\sigma_{v}}=(0,\sigma_{u};0,\sigma_{v};0) where σu,σv∈{−1,+1}\sigma_{u},\sigma_{v}\in\{-1,+1\}.

Proof.

Equation (1) shows that the only points with non-trivial isotropy are the points with u=v=z=0u=v=z=0. Then, the relations u​u¯+u32=1u\bar{u}+u_{3}^{2}=1 and v​v¯+v32=1v\bar{v}+v_{3}^{2}=1 imply that u3=±1u_{3}=\pm 1 and v3=±1v_{3}=\pm 1. ∎

The four fixed points given in Proposition 3 satisfy

F​(fpσu,σv)=((δ1−ω)​σu−2​g2δ2−δ1​σu​σv,(δ2−ω)​σv+2​g2δ2−δ1​σu​σv,σu+σv).F(\mathrm{fp}_{\sigma_{u},\sigma_{v}})=\Bigl((\delta_{1}-\omega)\sigma_{u}-\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\sigma_{u}\sigma_{v},(\delta_{2}-\omega)\sigma_{v}+\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\sigma_{u}\sigma_{v},\sigma_{u}+\sigma_{v}\Bigr). (9)

Notice that for k∉{−2,0,2}k\not\in\{-2,0,2\}, the set K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k) does not contain any fixed points of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K}. This implies that the reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is a smooth manifold for k∈(−2,0)∪(0,2)∪(2,∞)k\in(-2,0)\cup(0,2)\cup(2,\infty). The topology of these reduced spaces is given in Proposition 20.

It will be useful in the following to reduce the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K} and obtain a two-degree-of-freedom Hamiltonian system on the reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}. The reduction of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action can be performed globally using algebraic invariants, that is, using the Hilbert basis for the given group action. Reduction using algebraic invariants is described in detail in Appendix A, where it is shown that it leads to a description of the four-dimensional reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} with 99 invariants that satisfy a number of syzygies which are not all independent, and with a non-standard Poisson structure. From this point of view, even though algebraic reduction has the benefit of being globally applicable, it can be cumbersome. Therefore, if we are interested only in a local description of the dynamics, e.g., near a relative equilibrium, it is more convenient to reduce the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action so that we are left with exactly 44 coordinates in the reduced space.

To this effect, we first introduce canonical coordinates (θu,u3;θv,v3;q,p)(\theta_{u},u_{3};\theta_{v},v_{3};q,p) on 𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2×𝐑2\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v}\times\mathbf{R}^{2}, where 𝐒∗,u,v2=𝐒u,v2∖{(0,0,±1)}\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u,v}=\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u,v}\setminus\{(0,0,\pm 1)\}, with

u=u1+i​u2=(1−u32)1/2​exp⁡(i​θu),v=v1+i​v2=(1−v32)1/2​exp⁡(i​θv).u=u_{1}+iu_{2}=(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\exp(i\theta_{u}),\ v=v_{1}+iv_{2}=(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\exp(i\theta_{v}).

The given coordinates are canonical with {θu,u3}={θv,v3}={q,p}=1\{\theta_{u},u_{3}\}=\{\theta_{v},v_{3}\}=\{q,p\}=1.

Let M∗=𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2×𝐑∗2M_{*}=\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v}\times\mathbf{R}^{2}_{*}, where 𝐑∗2=𝐑2∖{(0,0)}\mathbf{R}^{2}_{*}=\mathbf{R}^{2}\setminus\{(0,0)\}. Then, the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K} on K−1​(k)∩M∗K^{-1}(k)\cap M_{*} is reduced by defining the section

s:𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2→K−1​(k)∩M∗:(θu,u3;θv,v3)↦(θu,u3;θv,v3;[2​(k−u3−v3)]1/2,0).s:\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v}\to K^{-1}(k)\cap M_{*}:(\theta_{u},u_{3};\theta_{v},v_{3})\mapsto(\theta_{u},u_{3};\theta_{v},v_{3};[2(k-u_{3}-v_{3})]^{1/2},0).

For any smooth 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} invariant function HH on M∗M_{*} the corresponding reduced function on 𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v} is given by H^=s∗​H\widehat{H}=s^{*}H. The functions H1H_{1}, H2H_{2} in (4) reduce to

H^1=(δ1−ω)​u3+2​g​(1−u32)1/2​(k−u3−v3)1/2​cos⁡θu−2​g2δ2−δ1​[(1−u32)1/2​(1−v32)1/2​cos⁡(θu−θv)+u3​v3],\displaystyle\begin{aligned} \widehat{H}_{1}&=(\delta_{1}-\omega)u_{3}+2g(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}(k-u_{3}-v_{3})^{1/2}\cos\theta_{u}\\ &\quad-\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\bigl[(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\cos(\theta_{u}-\theta_{v})+u_{3}v_{3}\bigr],\end{aligned} (10a)
H^2=(δ2−ω)​v3+2​g​(1−v32)1/2​(k−u3−v3)1/2​cos⁡θv+2​g2δ2−δ1​[(1−u32)1/2​(1−v32)1/2​cos⁡(θu−θv)+u3​v3].\displaystyle\begin{aligned} \widehat{H}_{2}&=(\delta_{2}-\omega)v_{3}+2g(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}(k-u_{3}-v_{3})^{1/2}\cos\theta_{v}\\ &\quad+\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\bigl[(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\cos(\theta_{u}-\theta_{v})+u_{3}v_{3}\bigr].\end{aligned} (10b)

4 Singularities of the Tavis-Cummings system

In this section, we parameterize the singularities of the integral map FF of the TC system and we determine their type for the STC system. Rank-0 singularities are discussed in Section 4.2, while rank-11 and rank-22 singularities are obtained in Sections 4.3 and 4.4, respectively. We summarize here the main results of this section for the STC system and we depict its set of critical values in Figure 1.

The integral map F=(H1,H2,K)F=(H_{1},H_{2},K) of the STC system has four rank-0 critical values which are given by Eq. (9) and lie on the boundary of the image of FF. We denote these by

𝔣1=F​(fp−1,−1)=(−32,32,−2),𝔣2=F​(fp+1,+1)=(−52,52,2),\displaystyle\mathfrak{f}_{1}=F(\mathrm{fp}_{-1,-1})=(-\tfrac{3}{2},\tfrac{3}{2},-2),\quad\mathfrak{f}_{2}=F(\mathrm{fp}_{+1,+1})=(-\tfrac{5}{2},\tfrac{5}{2},2),
𝔣3=F​(fp−1,+1)=(52,−32,0),𝔣4=F​(fp+1,−1)=(32,−52,0).\displaystyle\mathfrak{f}_{3}=F(\mathrm{fp}_{-1,+1})=(\tfrac{5}{2},-\tfrac{3}{2},0),\quad\mathfrak{f}_{4}=F(\mathrm{fp}_{+1,-1})=(\tfrac{3}{2},-\tfrac{5}{2},0).

The rank-11 focus-focus-regular critical values form four threads ℓj\ell_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4 in the interior of the image of FF that meet at a degenerate rank-11 critical value

𝔠∗=(h1∗,h2∗,k∗)=(2−3⋅22/3,−2+3⋅22/3,116​(12⋅22/3−1)),\mathfrak{c}_{*}=(h_{1}^{*},h_{2}^{*},k^{*})=\Bigl(2-3\cdot 2^{2/3},-2+3\cdot 2^{2/3},\frac{1}{16}(12\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)\Bigr),

that we call the central critical value. Each one of the threads ℓj\ell_{j}, j=2,3,4j=2,3,4, connects the boundary point 𝔣j\mathfrak{f}_{j} to the central critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}. The thread ℓ1\ell_{1} starts at 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} and meets the boundary of the image of FF at a point with K=16.0625K=16.0625, corresponding to a supercritical Hamiltonian Hopf bifurcation [36], and then continues along the boundary as the elliptic-elliptic-regular thread ℓ5\ell_{5}. Each critical value cc along the threads ℓj\ell_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4, has focus-focus-regular type, and thus the corresponding fiber F−1​(c)F^{-1}(c) is homeomorphic to the product of a two-dimensional pinched torus and 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1}. The central critical value corresponds to a fiber F−1​(𝔠∗)F^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*}) which is homeomorphic to 𝐒2×𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{1} and contains an 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} orbit of degenerate singularities, see Proposition 16.

The boundary of the image of FF is the closure of the set of rank-22 critical values. Besides the rank-22 critical values, it contains the fixed points 𝔣j\mathfrak{f}_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4, and the remaining four threads of rank-11 critical values of elliptic-elliptic-regular type denoted by ℓj\ell_{j}, j=5,…,8j=5,\dots,8.

4.1 Singularities in integrable Hamiltonian systems

Consider an integrable Hamiltonian system F=(F1,…,Fn):M2​n→𝐑nF=(F_{1},\dots,F_{n}):M^{2n}\to\mathbf{R}^{n}, and denote by XFjX_{F_{j}}, j=1,…,nj=1,\dots,n, the corresponding Hamiltonian vector fields. A point c∈Mc\in M is a rank-rr singular point (singularity; critical point) of FF if rank⁡D​F​(c)=r<n\operatorname{rank}DF(c)=r<n. The set CC of singular points of FF admits a natural stratification based on the rank. In particular, CC is stratified by the disjoint sets Cr={c∈M:rank⁡D​F​(c)=r<n}C_{r}=\{c\in M:\operatorname{rank}DF(c)=r<n\} with r=0,1,…,n−1r=0,1,\dots,n-1.

Define

L=span⁡{XF1​(c),…,XFn​(c)}⊆Tc​M.L=\operatorname{span}\{X_{F_{1}}(c),\dots,X_{F_{n}}(c)\}\subseteq T_{c}M.

Then, the condition that cc is a rank-rr singular point is equivalent to dimL=r<n\dim L=r<n and LL is an isotropic subspace of Tc​MT_{c}M. In this case, there are linear combinations Gj​(x)=∑i=1nki​(Fi​(x)−Fi​(c))G_{j}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}k_{i}(F_{i}(x)-F_{i}(c)), j=1,…,nj=1,\dots,n, with ki∈𝐑k_{i}\in\mathbf{R}, such that Gj​(c)=0G_{j}(c)=0 for j=1,…,nj=1,\dots,n, XG1​(c)=⋯=XGn−r​(c)=0X_{G_{1}}(c)=\cdots=X_{G_{n-r}}(c)=0, and XGj​(c)≠0X_{G_{j}}(c)\neq 0 for j=n−r+1,…,nj=n-r+1,\dots,n.

A rank-rr singular point c∈Mc\in M is non-degenerate if for some λ1,…,λn−r∈𝐑\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{n-r}\in\mathbf{R} the linear combination

λ1​D​XG1​(c)+⋯+λn−r​D​XGn−r​(c)|Lω/L,\lambda_{1}DX_{G_{1}}(c)+\dots+\lambda_{n-r}DX_{G_{n-r}}(c)\bigl|_{L^{\omega}/L},

has 2​(n−r)2(n-r) distinct non-zero eigenvalues, where D​XGj​(c)DX_{G_{j}}(c) is the linearization at cc of the vector field XGjX_{G_{j}}, and Lω⊇LL^{\omega}\supseteq L is the symplectic orthogonal of LL, cf. [10, Definition 1.24]. A point b∈𝐑nb\in\mathbf{R}^{n} is a critical value of FF if F−1​(b)F^{-1}(b) contains any singular points of FF. The bifurcation diagram ℬ​𝒟\mathcal{BD} associated to FF is the set of critical values of FF, that is, ℬ​𝒟=F​(C)\mathcal{BD}=F(C).

In the case of the two-spin Tavis-Cummings system, where n=3n=3, a singular point can have rank rr equal to 0, 11, or 22. We consider these cases separately in the following subsections.

4.2 Rank-0 singularities

If r=rank⁡D​F​(c)=0r=\operatorname{rank}DF(c)=0, then XFi​(c)=0X_{F_{i}}(c)=0, for i=1,2,3i=1,2,3. In this case, L={0}L=\{0\} and Lω/L=Tc​ML^{\omega}/L=T_{c}M. Therefore, cc is a non-degenerate singular point, if for some λ1,λ2,λ3∈𝐑\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2},\lambda_{3}\in\mathbf{R}, the linearized vector field

λ1​D​XF1​(c)+λ2​D​XF2​(c)+λ3​D​XF3​(c)\lambda_{1}DX_{F_{1}}(c)+\lambda_{2}DX_{F_{2}}(c)+\lambda_{3}DX_{F_{3}}(c)

has 66 distinct non-zero eigenvalues. This implies that the eigenvalues can combine in one of the following types, where αj∈𝐑∖{0}\alpha_{j}\in\mathbf{R}\setminus\{0\}, and the eigenvalues are distinct:

EEE:

elliptic-elliptic-elliptic, with eigenvalues ±i​α1\pm i\alpha_{1}, ±i​α2\pm i\alpha_{2}, ±i​α3\pm i\alpha_{3}.

EEH:

elliptic-elliptic-hyperbolic, with eigenvalues ±i​α1\pm i\alpha_{1}, ±i​α2\pm i\alpha_{2}, ±α3\pm\alpha_{3}.

EHH:

elliptic-hyperbolic-hyperbolic, with eigenvalues ±i​α1\pm i\alpha_{1}, ±α2\pm\alpha_{2}, ±α3\pm\alpha_{3}.

HHH:

hyperbolic-hyperbolic-hyperbolic, with eigenvalues ±α1\pm\alpha_{1}, ±α2\pm\alpha_{2}, ±α3\pm\alpha_{3}.

EFF:

elliptic-focus-focus, with eigenvalues ±i​α1\pm i\alpha_{1}, ±α2±i​α3\pm\alpha_{2}\pm i\alpha_{3}.

HFF:

hyperbolic-focus-focus, with eigenvalues ±α1\pm\alpha_{1}, ±α2±i​α3\pm\alpha_{2}\pm i\alpha_{3}.

In the Tavis-Cummings system, rank-0 singular points satisfy XK​(c)=0X_{K}(c)=0. Therefore, such points are the fixed points of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action which as we have seen in Section 3 are given by (u,u3;v,v3;z)=(0,±1;0,±1;0)(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z)=(0,\pm 1;0,\pm 1;0). The type of the rank-0 singularities of the STC system is given in the following result.

Proposition 4.

In the STC system, the rank-0 singularity (0,−1;0,−1;0)(0,-1;0,-1;0) is EEE (elliptic-elliptic-elliptic), while the rank-0 singularities (0,1;0,−1;0)(0,1;0,-1;0), (0,−1;0,1;0)(0,-1;0,1;0), and (0,1;0,1;0)(0,1;0,1;0) are EFF (elliptic-focus-focus).

Proof.

It is sufficient to compute the eigenvalues of D​XH1​(c)DX_{H_{1}}(c). We consider local charts (u,v,z)(u,v,z) near each of the four singular points (0,s1;0,s2;0)(0,s_{1};0,s_{2};0) with s1,s2∈{−1,+1}s_{1},s_{2}\in\{-1,+1\}. In each of these charts we have u3=s1​(1−u​u¯)1/2u_{3}=s_{1}(1-u\bar{u})^{1/2} and v3=s2​(1−v​v¯)1/2v_{3}=s_{2}(1-v\bar{v})^{1/2} and we compute in coordinates (u,u¯;v,v¯;z,z¯)(u,\bar{u};v,\bar{v};z,\bar{z}) that

D​XH1=[−2​i​s2−i202​i​s10−2​s1002​i​s2+i20−2​i​s10−2​s12​i​s20−2​i​s10000−2​i​s202​i​s100−2000000−20000].DX_{H_{1}}=\begin{bmatrix}-2is_{2}-\frac{i}{2}&0&2is_{1}&0&-\sqrt{2}s_{1}&0\\ 0&2is_{2}+\frac{i}{2}&0&-2is_{1}&0&-\sqrt{2}s_{1}\\ 2is_{2}&0&-2is_{1}&0&0&0\\ 0&-2is_{2}&0&2is_{1}&0&0\\ -\sqrt{2}&0&0&0&0&0\\ 0&-\sqrt{2}&0&0&0&0\\ \end{bmatrix}.

We find that for s1=s2=−1s_{1}=s_{2}=-1 the eigenvalues are ±4​i\pm 4i, ±14​(17+1)​i\pm\frac{1}{4}(\sqrt{17}+1)i, ±14​(17−1)​i\pm\frac{1}{4}(\sqrt{17}-1)i, and the singularity is EEE. For s1=s2=1s_{1}=s_{2}=1 the eigenvalues are ±4​i\pm 4i, 14​(±15±i)\frac{1}{4}(\pm\sqrt{15}\pm i), and the singularity is EFF. Finally, for s1=1s_{1}=1, s2=−1s_{2}=-1 and for s1=−1s_{1}=-1, s2=1s_{2}=1 the roots of the characteristic polynomial can be computed exactly but the resulting expressions are cumbersome. We give here the approximate values ±1.095​i\pm 1.095i, ±1.89±0.298​i\pm 1.89\pm 0.298i for s1=1s_{1}=1, s2=−1s_{2}=-1, and ±2.42​i\pm 2.42i, ±0.854±0.961​i\pm 0.854\pm 0.961i for s1=−1s_{1}=-1, s2=1s_{2}=1. In both cases, the singularity has type EFF. ∎

4.3 Rank-1 singularities

We give a parameterization of rank-11 singularities of the general TC system.

Proposition 5.

The rank-11 singularities of the Tavis-Cummings system are parameterized as

z​z¯=2​g2x2−(x+y−(δ1−ω))22​g2=2​g2y2−(x+y−(δ2−ω))22​g2,u=x​i−2​g​z,v=y​i−2​g​z,u3=−x2​g2​(x+y−(δ1−ω)),v3=−y2​g2​(x+y−(δ2−ω)).\begin{gathered}z\bar{z}=\frac{2g^{2}}{x^{2}}-\frac{(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega))^{2}}{2g^{2}}=\frac{2g^{2}}{y^{2}}-\frac{(x+y-(\delta_{2}-\omega))^{2}}{2g^{2}},\qquad u=\frac{xi}{-\sqrt{2}g}z,\quad v=\frac{yi}{-\sqrt{2}g}z,\\ u_{3}=-\frac{x}{2g^{2}}(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega)),\quad v_{3}=-\frac{y}{2g^{2}}(x+y-(\delta_{2}-\omega)).\end{gathered} (11)

The corresponding critical values are parameterized as

hc\displaystyle h^{c} =−12​g2​((x+y)2−x​(δ1−ω)−y​(δ2−ω))+g2x2−(x+y−(δ1−ω))24​g2,\displaystyle=-\frac{1}{2g^{2}}((x+y)^{2}-x(\delta_{1}-\omega)-y(\delta_{2}-\omega))+\frac{g^{2}}{x^{2}}-\frac{(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega))^{2}}{4g^{2}}, (12a)
h1c\displaystyle h_{1}^{c} =−2​g2​(y−δ2+δ1)(δ2−δ1)​x−x2​(x+y−(δ1−ω))2​g2,\displaystyle=-\frac{2g^{2}(y-\delta_{2}+\delta_{1})}{(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})x}-\frac{x^{2}(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega))}{2g^{2}}, (12b)
h2c\displaystyle h_{2}^{c} =2​g2​(x+δ2−δ1)(δ2−δ1)​y−y2​(x+y−(δ2−ω))2​g2,\displaystyle=\frac{2g^{2}(x+\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})}{(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})y}-\frac{y^{2}(x+y-(\delta_{2}-\omega))}{2g^{2}}, (12c)

where xx, y∈𝐑y\in\mathbf{R} satisfy

4​g4​(x2−y2)+(δ2−δ1)​x2​y2​(2​(x+y)−(δ1+δ2−2​ω))=0,4g^{4}(x^{2}-y^{2})+(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})x^{2}y^{2}(2(x+y)-(\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}-2\omega))=0, (13)

and are constrained by the conditions −1≤u3≤1-1\leq u_{3}\leq 1 and −1≤v3≤1-1\leq v_{3}\leq 1.

Proof.

If cc is a rank-11 singularity of FF then we must have XK​(c)≠0X_{K}(c)\neq 0. Therefore, to find rank-11 singularities we require that XH1=x​XKX_{H_{1}}=xX_{K} and XH2=y​XKX_{H_{2}}=yX_{K} for some x,y∈𝐑x,y\in\mathbf{R} and we make use of Eq. (3), Eq. (6), and Eq. (7).

First, XH1​(z)=x​XK​(z)X_{H_{1}}(z)=xX_{K}(z) and XH2​(z)=y​XK​(z)X_{H_{2}}(z)=yX_{K}(z) give

−2​g​u=x​i​z,−2​g​v=y​i​z.-\sqrt{2}gu=xiz,\ -\sqrt{2}gv=yiz.

Notice that these relations directly give u​v¯−u¯​v=0u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v=0, u​z¯+u¯​z=0u\bar{z}+\bar{u}z=0, v​z¯+v¯​z=0v\bar{z}+\bar{v}z=0 and thus XH1​(u3)=XH1​(v3)=XH2​(u3)=XH2​(v3)=0X_{H_{1}}(u_{3})=X_{H_{1}}(v_{3})=X_{H_{2}}(u_{3})=X_{H_{2}}(v_{3})=0. Since z=0z=0 also implies u=v=0u=v=0, corresponding to rank-0 singularities, we have z≠0z\neq 0.

The equations XH1​(v)=x​XK​(v)X_{H_{1}}(v)=xX_{K}(v) and XH2​(u)=y​XK​(u)X_{H_{2}}(u)=yX_{K}(u) give after substituting u,vu,v and dividing by z≠0z\neq 0 the same equation

−2​g2δ2−δ1​(y​u3−x​v3)=x​y.-\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(yu_{3}-xv_{3})=xy.

We then consider the equations XH1​(u)=x​XK​(u)X_{H_{1}}(u)=xX_{K}(u) and XH2​(v)=y​XK​(v)X_{H_{2}}(v)=yX_{K}(v). In the same way as above, we obtain

(δ1−ω)​x−2​g2​u3+2​g2δ2−δ1​(y​u3−x​v3)=x2,\displaystyle(\delta_{1}-\omega)x-2g^{2}u_{3}+\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(yu_{3}-xv_{3})=x^{2},
(δ2−ω)​y−2​g2​v3+2​g2δ2−δ1​(y​u3−x​v3)=y2,\displaystyle(\delta_{2}-\omega)y-2g^{2}v_{3}+\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(yu_{3}-xv_{3})=y^{2},

which can be solved for u3u_{3}, v3v_{3} to obtain

u3=−x2​g2​(x+y−(δ1−ω)),v3=−y2​g2​(x+y−(δ2−ω)).\displaystyle u_{3}=-\frac{x}{2g^{2}}(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega)),\ v_{3}=-\frac{y}{2g^{2}}(x+y-(\delta_{2}-\omega)).

Since −1≤u3≤1-1\leq u_{3}\leq 1 and −1≤v3≤1-1\leq v_{3}\leq 1, these equations give constraints for x,yx,y.

Moreover, we have

z​z¯\displaystyle z\bar{z} =2​g2x2​u​u¯=2​g2x2​(1−u32)=2​g2x2−(x+y−(δ1−ω))22​g2,\displaystyle=\frac{2g^{2}}{x^{2}}u\bar{u}=\frac{2g^{2}}{x^{2}}(1-u_{3}^{2})=\frac{2g^{2}}{x^{2}}-\frac{(x+y-(\delta_{1}-\omega))^{2}}{2g^{2}},
z​z¯\displaystyle z\bar{z} =2​g2y2​v​v¯=2​g2y2​(1−v32)=2​g2y2−(x+y−(δ2−ω))22​g2.\displaystyle=\frac{2g^{2}}{y^{2}}v\bar{v}=\frac{2g^{2}}{y^{2}}(1-v_{3}^{2})=\frac{2g^{2}}{y^{2}}-\frac{(x+y-(\delta_{2}-\omega))^{2}}{2g^{2}}.

Note that there is an arbitrariness of a phase as a result of the fact that rank-11 singularities belong to orbits of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K}.

The equality of the two expressions for z​z¯z\bar{z} gives the following implicit relation between xx and yy

4​g4​(x2−y2)+(δ2−δ1)​x2​y2​(2​(x+y)−(δ1+δ2−2​ω))=0.4g^{4}(x^{2}-y^{2})+(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})x^{2}y^{2}(2(x+y)-(\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}-2\omega))=0.

Substituting the obtained expressions for z​z¯z\bar{z}, u3u_{3}, v3v_{3}, uu, vv into Eq. (2) for KK and Eq. (4) for H1H_{1}, H2H_{2} we then obtain the parameterization in Eq. (12). ∎

Remark 6.

The proof of Proposition 5 is inspired by the discussion of rank-11 singularities in [5, Sec. 8.2] for the Tavis-Cummings system but does not make use of the spectral Lax pair formalism. In the spectral Lax pair formalism, the double roots of the spectral curve are not directly associated with physical quantities, and may lead to “non-physical” critical values. In [5] this issue is addressed by observing that for rank-11 singularities we have XH1=x​XKX_{H_{1}}=xX_{K}, XH2=y​XKX_{H_{2}}=yX_{K} for some real numbers xx, yy and showing that xx, yy must satisfy certain constraints. This is also the starting point for our approach in the proof of Proposition 5 and it is sufficient for producing a full parameterization of rank-11 singularities without using the spectral Lax pair.

Proposition 5 gives a parameterization of rank-11 singularities for the general TC system, except that we have an implicit relation between xx and yy in Eq. (13). For the STC system, this implicit relation can be simplified and we can obtain the parameterization of rank-11 singularities given in the following Proposition. The rank-11 singularities of the STC system are shown in Figure 1.

Proposition 7.

The rank-11 singularities of the special Tavis-Cummings system are parameterized as

z​z¯=2(a+b)2−18​(1−4​a)2,u=a−b2​i​z,v=a+b2​i​z,u3=−12​(a−b)​(2​a+12),v3=−12​(a+b)​(2​a−12),\begin{gathered}z\bar{z}=\frac{2}{(a+b)^{2}}-\frac{1}{8}(1-4a)^{2},\qquad u=\frac{a-b}{\sqrt{2}i}z,\quad v=\frac{a+b}{\sqrt{2}i}z,\\ u_{3}=-\frac{1}{2}(a-b)\left(2a+\frac{1}{2}\right),\quad v_{3}=-\frac{1}{2}(a+b)\left(2a-\frac{1}{2}\right),\end{gathered} (14)

where we have the following possibilities for how aa depends on bb, and the corresponding range of values of bb:

  1. ℓ1\ell_{1}:

    a=0a=0, 1/4<b<22/31/4<b<2^{2/3};

  2. ℓ2\ell_{2}:

    a=0a=0, 22/3<b<42^{2/3}<b<4;

  3. ℓ3\ell_{3}:

    a=−(b2−2​b1/2)1/2a=-(b^{2}-2b^{1/2})^{1/2}, 22/3<b<bmax2^{2/3}<b<b^{\max};

  4. ℓ4\ell_{4}:

    a=(b2−2​b1/2)1/2a=(b^{2}-2b^{1/2})^{1/2}, 22/3<b<bmax2^{2/3}<b<b^{\max};

  5. ℓ5\ell_{5}:

    a=0a=0, 0<b<1/40<b<1/4;

  6. ℓ6\ell_{6}:

    a=0a=0, −4<b<0-4<b<0;

  7. ℓ7\ell_{7}:

    a=(b2+2​b1/2)1/2a=(b^{2}+2b^{1/2})^{1/2}, 0<b<1/40<b<1/4;

  8. ℓ8\ell_{8}:

    a=−(b2+2​b1/2)1/2a=-(b^{2}+2b^{1/2})^{1/2}, 0<b<1/40<b<1/4;

  9. 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}:

    a=0a=0, b=22/3b=2^{2/3};

  10. hh\mathrm{hh}:

    a=0a=0, b=1/4b=1/4.

Here, bmaxb^{\max} is the unique real solution of the equation 16​b3−8​b2+b−64=016b^{3}-8b^{2}+b-64=0, given in closed form by

bmax=112​(2+(3455−48​5181)1/3+(3455+48​5181)1/3)≈1.7583.b^{\max}=\frac{1}{12}\Bigl(2+\bigl(3455-48\sqrt{5181}\bigr)^{1/3}+\bigl(3455+48\sqrt{5181}\bigr)^{1/3}\Bigr)\approx 1.7583.

The corresponding critical values are parameterized in all cases as

kc\displaystyle k^{c} =12​(b−4​a2)+1(a−b)2−116​(1+4​a)2,\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}(b-4a^{2})+\frac{1}{(a-b)^{2}}-\frac{1}{16}(1+4a)^{2}, (15a)
h1c\displaystyle h_{1}^{c} =2−14​(1+4​a)​(a−b)2+2​1−2​aa−b,\displaystyle=2-\frac{1}{4}(1+4a)(a-b)^{2}+2\,\frac{1-2a}{a-b}, (15b)
h2c\displaystyle h_{2}^{c} =−2+14​(1−4​a)​(a+b)2+2​1+2​aa+b.\displaystyle=-2+\frac{1}{4}(1-4a)(a+b)^{2}+2\,\frac{1+2a}{a+b}. (15c)
Proof.

In the STC system we have δ1+δ2=2​ω\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}=2\omega. Then Eq. (13) becomes

2​(x+y)​[2​g4​(x−y)+(δ2−δ1)​x2​y2]=0.2(x+y)[2g^{4}(x-y)+(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})x^{2}y^{2}]=0.

Defining x=a−bx=a-b, y=a+by=a+b, the last equation gives

a​((δ2−δ1)​(a2−b2)2−4​g4​b)=0,a((\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})(a^{2}-b^{2})^{2}-4g^{4}b)=0,

with solutions a=0a=0, or a2=b2±2​g2​b/(δ2−δ1)a^{2}=b^{2}\pm 2g^{2}\sqrt{b/(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})}. For the STC system we have g=1g=1 and δ2−δ1=1\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}=1. Therefore, the solutions become

a=0,b∈𝐑,a=±(b2+2​b1/2)1/2,b≥0,a=±(b2−2​b1/2)1/2,b≥22/3.a=0,\ b\in\mathbf{R},\qquad a=\pm(b^{2}+2b^{1/2})^{1/2},\ b\geq 0,\qquad a=\pm(b^{2}-2b^{1/2})^{1/2},\ b\geq 2^{2/3}.

Substituting x=a−bx=a-b, y=a+by=a+b in Eq. (11) and Eq. (12) we obtain Eq. (14) and Eq. (15), respectively.

We consider now the constraints −1≤u3≤1-1\leq u_{3}\leq 1, −1≤v3≤1-1\leq v_{3}\leq 1. For the solution a=0a=0 we obtain u3=v3=b/4u_{3}=v_{3}=b/4. Therefore, the constraints give −4≤b≤4-4\leq b\leq 4. However, the values b=±4b=\pm 4 correspond to the rank-0 singularities and are excluded, as is the value b=0b=0 where z​z¯z\bar{z} is not defined. We denote by ℓ6\ell_{6} the case a=0a=0, b∈(−4,0)b\in(-4,0). We split the case a=0a=0, b∈(0,4)b\in(0,4) to several subcases: ℓ5\ell_{5} for b∈(0,1/4)b\in(0,1/4), hh\mathrm{hh} for b=1/4b=1/4, ℓ1\ell_{1} for b∈(1/4,22/3)b\in(1/4,2^{2/3}), 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} for b=22/3b=2^{2/3}, and ℓ2\ell_{2} for b∈(22/3,4)b\in(2^{2/3},4). The reason for this is that hh\mathrm{hh} and 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} correspond to degenerate singularities.

For the solutions ℓ3\ell_{3}, ℓ4\ell_{4} with a=∓(b2−2​b1/2)1/2a=\mp(b^{2}-2b^{1/2})^{1/2} we find that they respectively give u3=−v3=−1u_{3}=-v_{3}=-1 and u3=−v3=1u_{3}=-v_{3}=1 for b=bmax≈1.7583b=b^{\max}\approx 1.7583. Therefore, we must have 22/3≤b≤bmax2^{2/3}\leq b\leq b^{\max}. The value b=22/3b=2^{2/3} gives a=0a=0 and corresponds to 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} and is excluded from this parameterization. This also shows that ℓ1\ell_{1}, ℓ2\ell_{2}, ℓ3\ell_{3}, ℓ4\ell_{4} meet at 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} with a=0a=0, b=22/3b=2^{2/3}. The value b=bmaxb=b^{\max} gives the rank-0 singularities u3=−v3=−1u_{3}=-v_{3}=-1, z=0z=0 for ℓ3\ell_{3} and u3=−v3=1u_{3}=-v_{3}=1, z=0z=0 for ℓ4\ell_{4}.

For the solutions ℓ7\ell_{7}, ℓ8\ell_{8} with a=±(b2+2​b1/2)1/2a=\pm(b^{2}+2b^{1/2})^{1/2} we find that they give −1≤u3≤0-1\leq u_{3}\leq 0 and −1≤v3≤0-1\leq v_{3}\leq 0 for 0≤b≤1/40\leq b\leq 1/4. The value b=0b=0 is excluded because it gives a=0a=0 and thus z​z¯z\bar{z} is not defined, while b=1/4b=1/4 is excluded because it gives the rank-0 singularity u3=v3=−1u_{3}=v_{3}=-1, z=0z=0. ∎

We now discuss the stability of the rank-11 singularities in the STC system.

Proposition 8.

The rank-11 singularities of the STC system given in Proposition 7 have the following types.

  1. (i)

    The families ℓ1\ell_{1}, ℓ2\ell_{2}, ℓ3\ell_{3}, ℓ4\ell_{4} are FFR (focus-focus-regular).

  2. (ii)

    The families ℓ5\ell_{5}, ℓ6\ell_{6}, ℓ7\ell_{7}, ℓ8\ell_{8} are EER (elliptic-elliptic-regular).

  3. (iii)

    The points hh\mathrm{hh} and 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} are degenerate.

We note here that in the STC system when a family is EER the corresponding critical values lie at the boundary of the image of the integral map FF, while FFR families form threads in the interior of the image of FF.

Proof.

To study the stability of the rank-11 singularities we perform a local reduction of the system, as described in Section 3. The corresponding reduced functions for the STC system are

H^1\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{1} =−12​u3−2​u3​v3+2​(1−u32)1/2​[(k−u3−v3)1/2​cos⁡θu−(1−v32)1/2​cos⁡(θu−θv)],\displaystyle=-\frac{1}{2}u_{3}-2u_{3}v_{3}+2(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\bigl[(k-u_{3}-v_{3})^{1/2}\cos\theta_{u}-(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\cos(\theta_{u}-\theta_{v})\bigr],
H^2\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{2} =12​v3+2​u3​v3+2​(1−v32)1/2​[(k−u3−v3)1/2​cos⁡θv+(1−u32)1/2​cos⁡(θu−θv)].\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}v_{3}+2u_{3}v_{3}+2(1-v_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\bigl[(k-u_{3}-v_{3})^{1/2}\cos\theta_{v}+(1-u_{3}^{2})^{1/2}\cos(\theta_{u}-\theta_{v})\bigr].

The stability of the rank-11 singularities is determined by analyzing the eigenvalues of the derivatives D​XH^1DX_{\widehat{H}_{1}}, D​XH^2DX_{\widehat{H}_{2}} evaluated at the singularity. For reasons of computational convenience we consider instead of XH^1X_{\widehat{H}_{1}} and XH^2X_{\widehat{H}_{2}}, their linear combinations

XH^+=XH^1+XH^2,XH^−=XH^1−XH^2,X_{\widehat{H}_{+}}=X_{\widehat{H}_{1}}+X_{\widehat{H}_{2}},\quad X_{\widehat{H}_{-}}=X_{\widehat{H}_{1}}-X_{\widehat{H}_{2}},

and their derivatives D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}}, D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}}. We report the results for each family of rank-11 singularities separately.

Families ℓ1\ell_{1}, ℓ2\ell_{2}, ℓ5\ell_{5}, ℓ6\ell_{6}. For these families, the characteristic polynomials for D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} are respectively

P1​(r)=1b4​(b2​r2−(b3−4))2,P2​(r)=1b4​(b2​r2−(b3−4)​(1−4​b))2.P_{1}(r)=\frac{1}{b^{4}}(b^{2}r^{2}-(b^{3}-4))^{2},\quad P_{2}(r)=\frac{1}{b^{4}}(b^{2}r^{2}-(b^{3}-4)(1-4b))^{2}.

The form of the characteristic polynomials implies that each matrix has at most two distinct eigenvalues with algebraic multiplicity 22 and they are either both real (±α)(\pm\alpha) or both imaginary (±i​β)(\pm i\beta). If −4<b<0-4<b<0 (ℓ6\ell_{6}), or if 0<b<1/40<b<1/4 (ℓ5\ell_{5}), then both D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} have imaginary eigenvalues and the singularity has type EER. If 1/4<b<22/31/4<b<2^{2/3} (ℓ1\ell_{1}) then D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} has imaginary eigenvalues and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} has real eigenvalues. In this case, the singularity has type FFR. If 22/3<b<42^{2/3}<b<4 (ℓ2\ell_{2}), then D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} has real eigenvalues and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} has imaginary eigenvalues. In this case, the singularity has again type FFR.

If b=1/4b=1/4, then D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} has imaginary eigenvalues, while D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} has four 0 eigenvalues; this degenerate point marks the transition from the EER family ℓ5\ell_{5} to the FFR family ℓ1\ell_{1}. Finally, if b=22/3b=2^{2/3}, then both matrices have four 0 eigenvalues; this is the most degenerate case corresponding to 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}.

Families ℓ3\ell_{3}, ℓ4\ell_{4}. For these families, the characteristic polynomials for D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} are respectively

P1​(r)\displaystyle P_{1}(r) =r4−2b1/2​(−b3/2+2)​(6​b+1)​r2+2b​(−b3/2+2)​(1+12​b−4​b3/2​(6​b−1)),\displaystyle=r^{4}-\frac{2}{b^{1/2}}(-b^{3/2}+2)(6b+1)r^{2}+\frac{2}{b}(-b^{3/2}+2)(1+12b-4b^{3/2}(6b-1)),
P2​(r)\displaystyle P_{2}(r) =r4−2b1/2​(−b3/2+2)​(32​b2−10​b−16​b1/2+1)​r2\displaystyle=r^{4}-\frac{2}{b^{1/2}}(-b^{3/2}+2)(32b^{2}-10b-16b^{1/2}+1)r^{2}
+2b​(−b3/2+2)​(4​b−1)2​(1+12​b−4​b3/2​(6​b−1)).\displaystyle\qquad\qquad+\frac{2}{b}(-b^{3/2}+2)(4b-1)^{2}(1+12b-4b^{3/2}(6b-1)).

For all b∈(22/3,bmax)b\in(2^{2/3},b^{\max}) the eigenvalues of both D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} are complex with non-zero real and imaginary parts, and the type of the singularity is FFR.

Families ℓ7\ell_{7}, ℓ8\ell_{8}. For these families, the characteristic polynomials for D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} are respectively

P1​(r)\displaystyle P_{1}(r) =r4+2b1/2​(b3/2+2)​(6​b+1)​r2+2b​(b3/2+2)​(1+12​b+4​b3/2​(6​b−1)),\displaystyle=r^{4}+\frac{2}{b^{1/2}}(b^{3/2}+2)(6b+1)r^{2}+\frac{2}{b}(b^{3/2}+2)(1+12b+4b^{3/2}(6b-1)),
P2​(r)\displaystyle P_{2}(r) =r4+2b1/2​(b3/2+2)​(32​b2−10​b+16​b1/2+1)​r2\displaystyle=r^{4}+\frac{2}{b^{1/2}}(b^{3/2}+2)(32b^{2}-10b+16b^{1/2}+1)r^{2}
+2b​(b3/2+2)​(4​b−1)2​(1+12​b+4​b3/2​(6​b−1)).\displaystyle\qquad\qquad+\frac{2}{b}(b^{3/2}+2)(4b-1)^{2}(1+12b+4b^{3/2}(6b-1)).

For all b∈(0,1/4)b\in(0,1/4) the eigenvalues of both D​XH^+DX_{\widehat{H}_{+}} and D​XH^−DX_{\widehat{H}_{-}} are imaginary and the type of the singularity is EER. ∎

4.4 Rank-2 singularities

The rank-22 critical values of the general TC system are given in the following result whose proof is presented in Appendix B. The rank-22 singularities for the STC system are depicted in Figure 1.

Proposition 9.

The rank-22 critical values of the general Tavis-Cummings system on K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k) are parameterized by

H1\displaystyle H_{1} =1δ2−δ1​[−kx2−C2​g2x2+Cx2​y+g2​(y2x2+2​xy−1)],\displaystyle=\frac{1}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\left[-\frac{k}{x^{2}}-\frac{C^{2}g^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{C}{x^{2}y}+g^{2}\Bigl(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+2\frac{x}{y}-1\Bigr)\right], (16a)
H2\displaystyle H_{2} =−1δ2−δ1​[−ky2−C2​g2y2+Cx​y2+g2​(x2y2+2​yx−1)],\displaystyle=-\frac{1}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}\left[-\frac{k}{y^{2}}-\frac{C^{2}g^{2}}{y^{2}}+\frac{C}{xy^{2}}+g^{2}\Bigl(\frac{x^{2}}{y^{2}}+2\frac{y}{x}-1\Bigr)\right], (16b)

where x,y∈𝐑x,y\in\mathbf{R} satisfy x−y=(δ2−δ1)​x​yx-y=(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})xy, and

C=12​g2​(δ1−ω)−12​g2​x=12​g2​(δ2−ω)−12​g2​y.\displaystyle C=\frac{1}{2g^{2}}(\delta_{1}-\omega)-\frac{1}{2g^{2}x}=\frac{1}{2g^{2}}(\delta_{2}-\omega)-\frac{1}{2g^{2}y}.

The parameter xx takes only those values for which the cubic polynomial P​(x,y;u3)=a3​(x,y)​u33+a2​(x,y)​u32+a1​(x,y)​u3+a0​(x,y)P(x,y;u_{3})=a_{3}(x,y)u_{3}^{3}+a_{2}(x,y)u_{3}^{2}+a_{1}(x,y)u_{3}+a_{0}(x,y) has two roots in [−1,1][-1,1]. Here,

a3​(x,y)\displaystyle a_{3}(x,y) =−4​g2​x2​y​(x−y)\displaystyle=-4g^{2}x^{2}y(x-y)
a2​(x,y)\displaystyle a_{2}(x,y) =−4​C2​g4​x2​y2+8​C​g2​x2​y−4​C​g2​x​y2−4​g2​K​x2​y2−(x−y)2\displaystyle=-4C^{2}g^{4}x^{2}y^{2}+8Cg^{2}x^{2}y-4Cg^{2}xy^{2}-4g^{2}Kx^{2}y^{2}-(x-y)^{2}
a1​(x,y)\displaystyle a_{1}(x,y) =2(2C3g4xy2−3C2g2xy+C2g2y2+2Cg4x3y2−2Cg4xy4+2Cg2Kxy2\displaystyle=2(2C^{3}g^{4}xy^{2}-3C^{2}g^{2}xy+C^{2}g^{2}y^{2}+2Cg^{4}x^{3}y^{2}-2Cg^{4}xy^{4}+2Cg^{2}Kxy^{2}
+Cx−Cy+g2x3y−g2x2y2+g2xy3−g2y4−Kxy+Ky2)\displaystyle\qquad+Cx-Cy+g^{2}x^{3}y-g^{2}x^{2}y^{2}+g^{2}xy^{3}-g^{2}y^{4}-Kxy+Ky^{2})
a0​(x,y)\displaystyle a_{0}(x,y) =−C4​g4​y2+2​C3​g2​y−2​C2​g4​x2​y2+2​C2​g4​y4−2​C2​g2​K​y2−C2−2​C​g2​x2​y\displaystyle=-C^{4}g^{4}y^{2}+2C^{3}g^{2}y-2C^{2}g^{4}x^{2}y^{2}+2C^{2}g^{4}y^{4}-2C^{2}g^{2}Ky^{2}-C^{2}-2Cg^{2}x^{2}y
−2​C​g2​y3+2​C​K​y−g4​x4​y2+2​g4​x2​y4−g4​y6+2​g2​K​x2​y2+2​g2​K​y4−K2​y2.\displaystyle\qquad-2Cg^{2}y^{3}+2CKy-g^{4}x^{4}y^{2}+2g^{4}x^{2}y^{4}-g^{4}y^{6}+2g^{2}Kx^{2}y^{2}+2g^{2}Ky^{4}-K^{2}y^{2}.

5 Hamiltonian monodromy

We consider the Hamiltonian monodromy of the STC system. First, we discuss the fundamental definitions and properties of Hamiltonian monodromy for 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems. Then, we give the Hamiltonian monodromy matrices for the special Tavis-Cummings system based on numerical calculations. Finally, we discuss the implications of these results for the reduced 2-DOF Hamiltonian systems on K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}.

5.1 Hamiltonian monodromy in 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian systems

Consider a 3-DOF integrable Hamiltonian system F:M→𝐑3F:M\to\mathbf{R}^{3}, denote by RR the set of regular values of FF, and assume for simplicity that each regular fiber F−1​(r)F^{-1}(r), r∈Rr\in R, is connected and compact and thus F−1​(r)≃𝐓3F^{-1}(r)\simeq\mathbf{T}^{3}. Recall that, for r0∈Rr_{0}\in R, the monodromy map

μ:π1​(R,r0)→Aut​(F−1​(r0)),\mu:\pi_{1}(R,r_{0})\to\mathrm{Aut}(F^{-1}(r_{0})),

maps each homotopy class γ\gamma with base point r0r_{0}, to an element μ​(γ)\mu(\gamma) in the group Aut​(F−1​(r0))\mathrm{Aut}(F^{-1}(r_{0})) of orientation preserving automorphisms of the fiber F−1​(r0)F^{-1}(r_{0}). The latter group is isomorphic to SL⁡(3,𝐙)\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}).

The element μ​(γ)∈SL⁡(3,𝐙)\mu(\gamma)\in\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) is defined via parallel transport of the period lattice of r0r_{0} along a representative of the homotopy class γ\gamma. We recall here the construction. Given a connected regular fiber F−1​(r)≃𝐓3F^{-1}(r)\simeq\mathbf{T}^{3}, the period lattice Λr\Lambda_{r} is defined as

Λr={𝑻=(T1,T2,T3)∈𝐑3:φH1T1∘φH2T2∘φKT3=idF−1​(r)}.\Lambda_{r}=\bigl\{\boldsymbol{T}=(T_{1},T_{2},T_{3})\in\mathbf{R}^{3}:\varphi_{H_{1}}^{T_{1}}\circ\varphi_{H_{2}}^{T_{2}}\circ\varphi_{K}^{T_{3}}=\mathrm{id}_{F^{-1}(r)}\bigr\}.

The period lattice Λr\Lambda_{r} is isomorphic to 𝐙3\mathbf{Z}^{3}. The period lattice bundle is the disjoint union

Λ=⨆r∈RΛr.\Lambda=\bigsqcup_{r\in R}\Lambda_{r}.

Over each simply connected open subset U⊆RU\subseteq R, the restriction of the period lattice bundle Λ|U\Lambda|_{U} is a trivial bundle, that is,

Λ|U=⨆r∈UΛr≃U×𝐙3.\Lambda|_{U}=\bigsqcup_{r\in U}\Lambda_{r}\simeq U\times\mathbf{Z}^{3}.

Therefore, there is a well defined notion of parallel transport of elements of the period lattice bundle along paths in RR.

Remark 10.

If 𝑻=(T1,T2,T3)∈Λr\boldsymbol{T}=(T_{1},T_{2},T_{3})\in\Lambda_{r}, then the vector field X𝑻=T1​XH1+T2​XH2+T3​XKX_{\boldsymbol{T}}=T_{1}X_{H_{1}}+T_{2}X_{H_{2}}+T_{3}X_{K} generates a period-11 flow φ\varphi on F−1​(r)F^{-1}(r) defined by φs=φH1s​T1∘φH2s​T2∘φKs​T3\varphi^{s}=\varphi_{H_{1}}^{sT_{1}}\circ\varphi_{H_{2}}^{sT_{2}}\circ\varphi_{K}^{sT_{3}}. Moreover, given a smooth section 𝑻:U→Λ|U:r↦𝑻​(r)\boldsymbol{T}:U\to\Lambda|_{U}:r\mapsto\boldsymbol{T}(r), the vector field X𝑻​(r)X_{\boldsymbol{T}(r)} is Hamiltonian with generating function 2​π​I2\pi I, where II is a (local) action coordinate for the system.

Given a loop γ\gamma, based at r0∈Rr_{0}\in R, the parallel transport of a period vector 𝑻∈Λr0\boldsymbol{T}\in\Lambda_{r_{0}} along γ\gamma results to a possibly different period vector 𝑻′∈Λr0\boldsymbol{T}^{\prime}\in\Lambda_{r_{0}}, and thus induces a 𝐙\mathbf{Z} linear map μ​(γ):Λr0→Λr0\mu(\gamma):\Lambda_{r_{0}}\to\Lambda_{r_{0}}.

Given a basis 𝑻1,𝑻2,𝑻3\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2},\boldsymbol{T}_{3} of Λr0\Lambda_{r_{0}}, the map μ​(γ)\mu(\gamma) is fully described by its action on the given basis. In particular, it can be described through a matrix M~​(γ)∈SL⁡(3,𝐙)\widetilde{M}(\gamma)\in\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) such that

𝑻′=μ​(γ)​(𝑻)=M~​(γ)​𝑻.\boldsymbol{T}^{\prime}=\mu(\gamma)(\boldsymbol{T})=\widetilde{M}(\gamma)\boldsymbol{T}.

Suppose that 𝑻i′=∑jmi​j​𝑻j\boldsymbol{T}_{i}^{\prime}=\sum_{j}m_{ij}\boldsymbol{T}_{j}, where mi​jm_{ij} are the elements of a matrix M​(γ)M(\gamma).

Lemma 11.

M​(γ)=M~​(γ)tM(\gamma)=\widetilde{M}(\gamma)^{t}.

Proof.

Write 𝑻=∑jaj​𝑻j\boldsymbol{T}=\sum_{j}a_{j}\boldsymbol{T}_{j} and 𝑻′=∑ibi​𝑻i\boldsymbol{T}^{\prime}=\sum_{i}b_{i}\boldsymbol{T}_{i}. The linearity of μ​(γ)\mu(\gamma) implies 𝑻′=∑jaj​𝑻j′\boldsymbol{T}^{\prime}=\sum_{j}a_{j}\boldsymbol{T}_{j}^{\prime}, and we find ∑j∑iaj​mj​i​𝑻i=∑ibi​𝑻i\sum_{j}\sum_{i}a_{j}m_{ji}\boldsymbol{T}_{i}=\sum_{i}b_{i}\boldsymbol{T}_{i}, implying bi=∑jmj​i​ajb_{i}=\sum_{j}m_{ji}a_{j}. However, by definition, bi=∑j[M​(γ)]i​j​ajb_{i}=\sum_{j}[M(\gamma)]_{ij}a_{j}, implying that the matrix M​(γ)M(\gamma) relating the two bases is the transpose of the matrix M~​(γ)\widetilde{M}(\gamma) expressing the automorphism μ​(γ)\mu(\gamma). ∎

Given two loops (or homotopy classes) γ\gamma, γ′\gamma^{\prime} based at r0∈Rr_{0}\in R we denote by γ⋅γ′\gamma\cdot\gamma^{\prime} the loop (or homotopy class) defined by first traversing γ\gamma and then γ′\gamma^{\prime}. Then the map γ↦M​(γ)\gamma\mapsto M(\gamma) induces a homomorphism from π1​(R,r0)\pi_{1}(R,r_{0}) to SL⁡(3,𝐙)\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) with the property that

M​(γ⋅γ′)=M​(γ)​M​(γ′).M(\gamma\cdot\gamma^{\prime})=M(\gamma)M(\gamma^{\prime}).
Lemma 12.

Suppose that {𝐓i}i=1,2,3\{\boldsymbol{T}_{i}\}_{i=1,2,3} and {𝐒i}i=1,2,3\{\boldsymbol{S}_{i}\}_{i=1,2,3} are bases of Λr0\Lambda_{r_{0}} satisfying 𝐒i=∑jAi​j​𝐓j\boldsymbol{S}_{i}=\sum_{j}A_{ij}\boldsymbol{T}_{j} with A=(Ai​j)∈SL⁡(3,𝐙)A=(A_{ij})\in\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}), and denote by M𝐓​(γ)M_{\boldsymbol{T}}(\gamma) and M𝐒​(γ)M_{\boldsymbol{S}}(\gamma) the monodromy matrices representing μ​(γ)\mu(\gamma) in each of the corresponding bases. Then

M𝑺​(γ)=A​M𝑻​(γ)​A−1.M_{\boldsymbol{S}}(\gamma)=AM_{\boldsymbol{T}}(\gamma)A^{-1}.
Proof.

Write M𝑻​(γ)=(Mi​j)M_{\boldsymbol{T}}(\gamma)=(M_{ij}) and M𝑺​(γ)=(Ni​j)M_{\boldsymbol{S}}(\gamma)=(N_{ij}). Then we have

μ​(γ)​(𝑺i)=∑jAi​j​μ​(γ)​(𝑻j)=∑j∑kAi​j​Mj​k​𝑻k=∑l(∑j∑kAi​j​Mj​k​(A−1)k​l)​𝑺l.\mu(\gamma)(\boldsymbol{S}_{i})=\sum_{j}A_{ij}\mu(\gamma)(\boldsymbol{T}_{j})=\sum_{j}\sum_{k}A_{ij}M_{jk}\boldsymbol{T}_{k}=\sum_{l}\Bigl(\sum_{j}\sum_{k}A_{ij}M_{jk}(A^{-1})_{kl}\Bigr)\boldsymbol{S}_{l}.

Since μ​(γ)​(𝒔i)=∑jNi​l​𝑺l\mu(\gamma)(\boldsymbol{s}_{i})=\sum_{j}N_{il}\boldsymbol{S}_{l}, we obtain

Ni​l=∑j∑kAi​j​Mj​k​(A−1)k​l,N_{il}=\sum_{j}\sum_{k}A_{ij}M_{jk}(A^{-1})_{kl},

that is, M𝑺​(γ)=A​M𝑻​(γ)​A−1M_{\boldsymbol{S}}(\gamma)=AM_{\boldsymbol{T}}(\gamma)A^{-1}. ∎

5.2 Hamiltonian monodromy in the special Tavis-Cummings system

We now specialize the discussion above to the special Tavis-Cummings system.

Proposition 13.

For each r0∈Rr_{0}\in R, where RR is the set of regular values of the special Tavis-Cummings system, the fundamental group π1​(R,r0)\pi_{1}(R,r_{0}) is isomorphic to the free group of three generators 𝐅3=𝐙∗𝐙∗𝐙\mathbf{F}_{3}=\mathbf{Z}*\mathbf{Z}*\mathbf{Z}.

Proof.

The set RR is homeomorphic to 𝐑3∖({x=0}∪{y=0})\mathbf{R}^{3}\setminus(\{x=0\}\cup\{y=0\}) and the latter deformation retracts to the unit sphere with four punctures, denoted by SS. In turn, SS deformation retracts to the open unit disk with three punctures, and that to the wedge sum of three circles, the fundamental group of which is isomorphic to 𝐅3\mathbf{F}_{3} [25, Example 1.21]. ∎

Since RR is path-connected we can write π1​(R)\pi_{1}(R) instead of π1​(R,r0)\pi_{1}(R,r_{0}). We construct representatives of four non-trivial homotopy classes of π1​(R)\pi_{1}(R) based at r0r_{0}. First, choose a value K=k1=1.8K=k_{1}=1.8 and consider the regular value r0=(2,1,k1)r_{0}=(2,1,k_{1}) of the integral map FF. Since K∗<k1<2K_{*}<k_{1}<2, the plane K=k1K=k_{1} contains two focus-focus-regular singularities ff1≈(−0.578466,0.578466,k1)\mathrm{f{}f}_{1}\approx(-0.578466,0.578466,k_{1}) and ff2≈(−1.74301,1.74301,k1)\mathrm{f{}f}_{2}\approx(-1.74301,1.74301,k_{1}), see Figure 2 (left). Then, choose a value K=k2=0.3K=k_{2}=0.3. Since 0<k2<K∗0<k_{2}<K_{*}, the plane K=k2K=k_{2} contains the focus-focus-regular singularities ff3≈(1.79368,−1.16404,k2)\mathrm{f{}f}_{3}\approx(1.79368,-1.16404,k_{2}) and ff4≈(1.16404,−1.79368,k2)\mathrm{f{}f}_{4}\approx(1.16404,-1.79368,k_{2}), see Figure 2 (center). Then, define loops γj\gamma_{j}, j=1,2,3,4j=1,2,3,4 in the following way. Each γj\gamma_{j} consists of a straight line joining r0r_{0} to the point ffj+(L,0,0)\mathrm{f{}f}_{j}+(L,0,0), followed by a circular path of radius LL centered at ffj\mathrm{f{}f}_{j} and parameterized by ffj+(L​cos⁡(2​π​s),L​sin⁡(2​π​s),0)\mathrm{f{}f}_{j}+(L\cos(2\pi s),L\sin(2\pi s),0), 0≤s≤10\leq s\leq 1, and finally followed by a straight line back to r0r_{0}. Notice that γ1\gamma_{1}, γ2\gamma_{2} lie on the plane K=k1K=k_{1}. The loops γj\gamma_{j}, j=1,2,3,4j=1,2,3,4 are shown in Figure 2.

The corresponding homotopy classes satisfy the relation

γ2⋅γ1=γ3⋅γ4.\gamma_{2}\cdot\gamma_{1}=\gamma_{3}\cdot\gamma_{4}.

Therefore, any three of these four homotopy classes can be chosen as generators of π1​(R)\pi_{1}(R). Moreover, notice that γ1⋅γ2≠γ2⋅γ1\gamma_{1}\cdot\gamma_{2}\neq\gamma_{2}\cdot\gamma_{1}, γ3⋅γ4≠γ4⋅γ3\gamma_{3}\cdot\gamma_{4}\neq\gamma_{4}\cdot\gamma_{3}, and γ2⋅γ1≠γ4⋅γ3\gamma_{2}\cdot\gamma_{1}\neq\gamma_{4}\cdot\gamma_{3}.

To compute the monodromy matrices M​(γj)M(\gamma_{j}), j=1,2,3,4j=1,2,3,4 for the four loops defined above we use the numerical procedure detailed in Appendix C. In short, we numerically compute a basis 𝑻1=(0,0,2​π)\boldsymbol{T}_{1}=(0,0,2\pi), 𝑻2\boldsymbol{T}_{2}, 𝑻3\boldsymbol{T}_{3} of the period lattice Λr0\Lambda_{r_{0}} on the fiber F−1​(r0)F^{-1}(r_{0}) and then we numerically continue this period lattice basis along a loop γ\gamma back to r0r_{0}. The numerical continuation along γ\gamma produces a new period lattice basis 𝑻1′=(0,0,2​π)\boldsymbol{T}_{1}^{\prime}=(0,0,2\pi), 𝑻2′\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{\prime}, 𝑻3′\boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{\prime}, and the corresponding Hamiltonian monodromy matrix is then given by

M=ℬ​(𝑻1′,𝑻2′,𝑻3′)​ℬ​(𝑻1,𝑻2,𝑻3)−1,M=\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1}^{\prime},\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{\prime},\boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{\prime})\,\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2},\boldsymbol{T}_{3})^{-1},

where ℬ​(𝑻1,𝑻2,𝑻3)\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2},\boldsymbol{T}_{3}) is the 3×33\times 3 matrix with rows given by the (row) vectors 𝑻1\boldsymbol{T}_{1}, 𝑻2\boldsymbol{T}_{2}, and 𝑻3\boldsymbol{T}_{3}.

Using this approach we computed the monodromy matrices for the loops γj\gamma_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4 as

M​(γ1)=[100011001],M​(γ2)=M​(γ4)=[1000100−11],M​(γ3)=[1000210−10].\displaystyle M(\gamma_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&1\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix},\ M(\gamma_{2})=M(\gamma_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&-1&1\end{bmatrix},\ M(\gamma_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&2&1\\ 0&-1&0\end{bmatrix}.

Notice that

M​(γ2)​M​(γ1)=M​(γ3)​M​(γ4)=[1000110−10],\displaystyle M(\gamma_{2})M(\gamma_{1})=M(\gamma_{3})M(\gamma_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&1\\ 0&-1&0\\ \end{bmatrix},

consistent with the relation γ2⋅γ1=γ3⋅γ4\gamma_{2}\cdot\gamma_{1}=\gamma_{3}\cdot\gamma_{4}.

Remark 14.

This result implies that the Lagrangian fibration induced by FF over its regular values is not globally trivializable [14]. Equivalently, the system does not admit globally defined action–angle coordinates. Moreover, the fact that there is no change of basis which can simultaneously bring the monodromy matrices into the form

[100010ab1]\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ a&b&1\\ \end{bmatrix}

confirms that the system does not have a global 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action. It is known, however, that near a thread of focus-focus-regular values there is a local 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action. We can read from the form of M​(γ1)M(\gamma_{1}) that the vector fields X𝑻1=2​π​XJX_{\boldsymbol{T}_{1}}=2\pi X_{J} and X𝑻3X_{\boldsymbol{T}_{3}} extend smoothly inside the disk bounded by γ1\gamma_{1} and generate a local 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action in the neighborhood of ℓ1\ell_{1}, see Remark 10. Similarly, we read from M​(γ2)=M​(γ4)M(\gamma_{2})=M(\gamma_{4}) that the vector fields X𝑻1X_{\boldsymbol{T}_{1}} and X𝑻2X_{\boldsymbol{T}_{2}} generate a local 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action in the neighborhood of ℓ2\ell_{2} and ℓ4\ell_{4}. Finally, we read from M​(γ3)M(\gamma_{3}) that the vector fields X𝑻1X_{\boldsymbol{T}_{1}} and X𝑻2+𝑻3X_{\boldsymbol{T}_{2}+\boldsymbol{T}_{3}} generate a local 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action in the neighborhood of ℓ3\ell_{3}.

5.3 Monodromy of 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} reduced 2-DOF Hamiltonian systems

Recall that for k∉{−2,0,2}k\not\in\{-2,0,2\} the reduced space K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k) is a smooth manifold, see Section 3 and Proposition 20. The corresponding reduced functions H^1\widehat{H}_{1}, H^2\widehat{H}_{2} define a reduced, 2-DOF integrable Hamiltonian system on K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}. We give here some brief observations about these reduced systems. Note that representative bifurcation diagrams for the cases discussed here are shown in Figure 1 as horizontal slices of the three-dimensional bifurcation diagram with the planes K=kK=k.

First, note that for −2<k<0-2<k<0 and for k>16.0625k>16.0625, the corresponding reduced system has no focus-focus points and is of toric type. For 2<k<16.06252<k<16.0625, the reduced system has a single focus-focus point and, therefore, it has a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action on the manifold K−1​(k)/𝐒1≃𝐒2×𝐒2K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}\simeq\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}.

We consider now the situation for 0<k<20<k<2. For 0<k<k∗0<k<k^{*}, where k∗≈1.12805k^{*}\approx 1.12805 corresponds to the central singularity, the reduced system has two focus-focus points and no global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action. The monodromy matrices around the two focus-focus points can be obtained by eliminating the first row and first column from the monodromy matrices computed in Section 5.2. We obtain

M^​(γ3)=[21−10],M^​(γ4)=[10−11].\displaystyle\widehat{M}(\gamma_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\ -1&0\end{bmatrix},\ \widehat{M}(\gamma_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ -1&1\end{bmatrix}.

For k∗<k<2k^{*}<k<2, the reduced system has again two focus-focus points and no global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action. The monodromy matrices are

M^​(γ1)=[1101],M^​(γ2)=[10−11].\displaystyle\widehat{M}(\gamma_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix},\ \widehat{M}(\gamma_{2})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ -1&1\end{bmatrix}.

At the transition point k=k∗k=k^{*} between the previous two cases, the reduced system has a single degenerate critical value in the interior of the image of the reduced integral map. This critical value corresponds to the A2A_{2} singularity and the corresponding reduced fiber is homeomorphic to 𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2}, see Proposition 16. As in the previous two cases, the reduced system her does not admit a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action. The monodromy matrix for a loop γ0\gamma_{0} around this critical value, obtained by homotopy from γ2⋅γ1\gamma_{2}\cdot\gamma_{1} or γ3⋅γ4\gamma_{3}\cdot\gamma_{4}, is

M^​(γ0)=[11−10],\displaystyle\widehat{M}(\gamma_{0})=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\ -1&0\\ \end{bmatrix},

for which we note that its eigenvalues are −e±2​π​i/3-e^{\pm 2\pi i/3} and therefore it cannot be brought into the standard form (10m1)\bigl(\begin{smallmatrix}1&0\\ m&1\end{smallmatrix}\bigr) by a change of basis.

6 𝑨𝟐\boldsymbol{A_{2}} singularity

In this section, we focus on the critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} at the meeting point of the four threads ℓj\ell_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4 of focus-focus-regular critical values, see Proposition 7. The corresponding fiber F−1​(𝔠∗)F^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*}) contains an 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} orbit of degenerate rank-11 critical points. Upon reducing the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action, this 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} orbit corresponds to a singularity c∗c_{*} in the reduced space. Following Thom’s terminology [35], the degenerate singularity c∗c_{*} acts as the organizing center of the bifurcations. We thus refer to 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*} as the central critical value, and to the corresponding critical point in the reduced space, c∗c_{*}, as the central singularity.

6.1 Reduction to normal form

Recall from Section 3 that the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action induced by the momentum KK on the subspace M∗=𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2×𝐑∗2M_{*}=\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v}\times\mathbf{R}^{2}_{*} can be reduced by introducing coordinates (θu,u3,θv,v3,k)(\theta_{u},u_{3},\theta_{v},v_{3},k). Here, we do not restrict attention to K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k), that is, we do not fix kk. Then, the integral map F=(H1,H2,K)F=(H_{1},H_{2},K) reduces to the map F^=(H^1,H^2,K^)\widehat{F}=(\widehat{H}_{1},\widehat{H}_{2},\widehat{K}), where H^1\widehat{H}_{1}, H^2\widehat{H}_{2} are given by Eq. (10) and K^​(θu,u3,θv,v3,k)=k\widehat{K}(\theta_{u},u_{3},\theta_{v},v_{3},k)=k.

It follows from the parameterization in Proposition 7 that the central singularity c∗c_{*} corresponds to the values θu∗=π\theta_{u}^{*}=\pi, θv∗=0\theta_{v}^{*}=0, u3∗=v3∗=1/(2⋅21/3)u_{3}^{*}=v_{3}^{*}=1/(2\cdot 2^{1/3}), and k∗=(12⋅22/3−1)/16k^{*}=(12\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)/16, and we recall that the corresponding critical value is

𝔠∗=F^​(c∗)=(2−3⋅2−2/3,−2+3⋅2−2/3,116​(12⋅22/3−1)).\mathfrak{c}_{*}=\widehat{F}(c_{*})=\Bigl(2-3\cdot 2^{-2/3},-2+3\cdot 2^{-2/3},\tfrac{1}{16}(12\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)\Bigr).

Let ℱ:𝐒u2×𝐒v2×𝐑2\mathcal{F}:\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\times\mathbf{R}^{2} be the integral map given by

ℱ=ψ∘F,\mathcal{F}=\psi\circ F,

where ψ:𝐑3→𝐑3\psi:\mathbf{R}^{3}\to\mathbf{R}^{3} is the diffeomorphism

ψ​(h1,h2,k)=(h1+h2,h2−h1+2​η​(k−k∗)(4⋅22/3−1)1/2,214/9​(k−k∗)(4⋅22/3−1)1/3),\psi(h_{1},h_{2},k)=\Bigl(h_{1}+h_{2},\,\frac{h_{2}-h_{1}+2\eta(k-k^{*})}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{1/2}},\,\frac{2^{14/9}(k-k^{*})}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{1/3}}\Bigr),

and

η​(k−k∗)=2−3⋅2−2/3−22/3​(k−k∗)+4⋅22/34⋅22/3−1​(k−k∗)2−32⋅22/3(4⋅22/3−1)2​(k−k∗)3.\eta(k-k^{*})=2-3\cdot 2^{-2/3}-2^{2/3}(k-k^{*})+\frac{4\cdot 2^{2/3}}{4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1}(k-k^{*})^{2}-\frac{32\cdot 2^{2/3}}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{2}}(k-k^{*})^{3}.

The function η​(k−k∗)\eta(k-k^{*}) is the cubic Taylor polynomial in k−k∗k-k^{*} of the expression obtained by substituting θu=θu∗\theta_{u}=\theta_{u}^{*}, u3=u3∗u_{3}=u_{3}^{*}, θv=θv∗\theta_{v}=\theta_{v}^{*}, v3=v3∗v_{3}=v_{3}^{*} in H^1\widehat{H}_{1}. It can be directly checked that ψ​(𝔠∗)=0\psi(\mathfrak{c}_{*})=0.

Since ψ\psi is a diffeomorphism, the singular Lagrangian fibrations induced by FF and ℱ\mathcal{F} are isomorphic. In particular, F−1​(v)=ℱ−1​(ψ​(v))F^{-1}(v)=\mathcal{F}^{-1}(\psi(v)). The integral map ℱ\mathcal{F} reduces under the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action to ℱ^=ψ∘F^\widehat{\mathcal{F}}=\psi\circ\widehat{F}. In the rest of this section we work with the reduced integral map ℱ^\widehat{\mathcal{F}} in a neighborhood of its critical value 0.

Theorem 15.

There exists a local fibered bundle isomorphism (Φ,ϕ)(\Phi,\phi) between the following local fibrations:

where f​(x,y,κ)=(y2+x3+κ​x,κ)f(x,y,\kappa)=(y^{2}+x^{3}+\kappa x,\kappa).

Proof.

Write ℱ^=(H^+,H^−,L^)\widehat{\mathcal{F}}=(\widehat{H}_{+},\widehat{H}_{-},\widehat{L}), replace θu=θu∗+ξu\theta_{u}=\theta_{u}^{*}+\xi_{u}, θv=θv∗+ξv\theta_{v}=\theta_{v}^{*}+\xi_{v}, u3=u3∗+χuu_{3}=u_{3}^{*}+\chi_{u}, v3=v3∗+χvv_{3}=v_{3}^{*}+\chi_{v}, and consider the Taylor expansions of H^+\widehat{H}_{+} and H^−\widehat{H}_{-} with respect to ξu\xi_{u}, ξv\xi_{v}, χu\chi_{u}, χv\chi_{v}, k−k∗k-k^{*}.

Up to linear terms the Taylor expansions are H^+(1)=H^−(1)=0\widehat{H}_{+}^{(1)}=\widehat{H}_{-}^{(1)}=0. The quadratic terms are given by

H^+(2)\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{+}^{(2)} =4⋅22/3−18⋅21/3​(ξu2−ξv2)+44⋅22/3−1​(k−k∗)​(χu−χv),\displaystyle=\frac{4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1}{8\cdot 2^{1/3}}(\xi_{u}^{2}-\xi_{v}^{2})+\frac{4}{4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1}(k-k^{*})(\chi_{u}-\chi_{v}),
H^−(2)\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{-}^{(2)} =(4⋅22/3−1)1/28⋅22/3​[(4−21/3)​(ξu2+ξv2)−8​ξu​ξv]+4(4⋅22/3−1)1/2​(k−k∗)​(χu+χv).\displaystyle=\frac{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{1/2}}{8\cdot 2^{2/3}}\bigl[(4-2^{1/3})(\xi_{u}^{2}+\xi_{v}^{2})-8\xi_{u}\xi_{v}\bigr]+\frac{4}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{1/2}}(k-k^{*})(\chi_{u}+\chi_{v}).

We observe that the quadratic terms for k=k∗k=k^{*} do not involve χu\chi_{u}, χv\chi_{v}. This implies, in particular, that the central singularity is degenerate.

We then compute the cubic terms H^+(3)\widehat{H}_{+}^{(3)}, H^−(3)\widehat{H}_{-}^{(3)} in the Taylor expansion of H^+\widehat{H}_{+}, H^−\widehat{H}_{-}, and we keep only these terms corresponding to the Newton polytope for each of these two functions. That is, we consider

H~+=H^+(2)+H~+(3),H~−=H^−(2)+H~−(3),\widetilde{H}_{+}=\widehat{H}_{+}^{(2)}+\widetilde{H}_{+}^{(3)},\quad\widetilde{H}_{-}=\widehat{H}_{-}^{(2)}+\widetilde{H}_{-}^{(3)},

where

H~+(3)\displaystyle\widetilde{H}_{+}^{(3)} =8(4⋅22/3−1)2​[(6−21/3)​(χv3−χu3)−6​χu​χv​(χu−χv)],\displaystyle=\frac{8}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{2}}\bigl[(6-2^{1/3})(\chi_{v}^{3}-\chi_{u}^{3})-6\chi_{u}\chi_{v}(\chi_{u}-\chi_{v})\bigr],
H~−(3)\displaystyle\widetilde{H}_{-}^{(3)} =−8(4⋅22/3−1)3/2​[(2−21/3)​(χu3+χv3)+6​(χu+χv)​χu​χv].\displaystyle=-\frac{8}{(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{3/2}}\bigl[(2-2^{1/3})(\chi_{u}^{3}+\chi_{v}^{3})+6(\chi_{u}+\chi_{v})\chi_{u}\chi_{v}\bigr].

The fibrations induced by ℱ^=(H^+,H^−,L^)\widehat{\mathcal{F}}=(\widehat{H}_{+},\widehat{H}_{-},\widehat{L}) and ℱ~=(H~+,H~−,L^)\widetilde{\mathcal{F}}=(\widetilde{H}_{+},\widetilde{H}_{-},\widehat{L}) are locally topologically equivalent in a neighborhood of the point (θu∗,u3∗,θv∗,v3∗,k∗)(\theta_{u}^{*},u_{3}^{*},\theta_{v}^{*},v_{3}^{*},k^{*}).

Consider complex coordinates (x,y)∈𝐂2(x,y)\in\mathbf{C}^{2}, write x=x1+i​x2x=x_{1}+ix_{2}, y=y1+i​y2y=y_{1}+iy_{2}, and define the function h:𝐂2×𝐑→𝐂h:\mathbf{C}^{2}\times\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{C} by

h​(x,y,κ)=y2+x3+κ​x.h(x,y,\kappa)=y^{2}+x^{3}+\kappa x.

Then, there is a linear change of coordinates (ξu,ξv)=(a11​y1+a12​y2,a21​y1+a22​y2)(\xi_{u},\xi_{v})=(a_{11}y_{1}+a_{12}y_{2},a_{21}y_{1}+a_{22}y_{2}), (χu,χv)=(b11​x1+b12​x2,b21​x1+b22​x2)(\chi_{u},\chi_{v})=(b_{11}x_{1}+b_{12}x_{2},b_{21}x_{1}+b_{22}x_{2}), and k−k∗=2−14/9​(4⋅22/3−1)1/3​κ≈0.594984​κk-k^{*}=2^{-14/9}(4\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)^{1/3}\kappa\approx 0.594984\,\kappa, such that in the new coordinates we have

H~+\displaystyle\widetilde{H}_{+} =y12−y22+x13−3​x1​x22+κ​x1=Re⁡h​(x,y,κ),\displaystyle=y_{1}^{2}-y_{2}^{2}+x_{1}^{3}-3x_{1}x_{2}^{2}+\kappa x_{1}=\operatorname{Re}h(x,y,\kappa),
H~−\displaystyle\widetilde{H}_{-} =2​y1​y2+3​x12​x2−x23+κ​x2=Im⁡h​(x,y,κ),\displaystyle=2y_{1}y_{2}+3x_{1}^{2}x_{2}-x_{2}^{3}+\kappa x_{2}=\operatorname{Im}h(x,y,\kappa),

that is,

h=H~++i​H~−.h=\widetilde{H}_{+}+i\widetilde{H}_{-}.

The numbers ai​j,bi​ja_{ij},b_{ij} in the linear change of coordinates are explicitly given as complicated radical expressions. We report here their approximate values:

a11=−a22≈1.49505,a21=−a12≈0.592494,\displaystyle a_{11}=-a_{22}\approx 1.49505,\ a_{21}=-a_{12}\approx 0.592494,
b11=−b21≈1.1239,b12=b22≈0.485921.\displaystyle b_{11}=-b_{21}\approx 1.1239,\ b_{12}=b_{22}\approx 0.485921.

Thus, the fibration induced by ℱ~=(H~+,H~−,L^)\widetilde{\mathcal{F}}=(\widetilde{H}_{+},\widetilde{H}_{-},\widehat{L}) on 𝐑5\mathbf{R}^{5} and the fibration induced by ff on 𝐂2×𝐑\mathbf{C}^{2}\times\mathbf{R} are isomorphic. Specifically, the fiber

ℱ~−1​(ε1,ε2,κ)\widetilde{\mathcal{F}}^{-1}\bigl(\varepsilon_{1},\,\varepsilon_{2},\,\kappa\bigr)

is, up to a linear coordinate transformation, exactly the fiber f−1​(ε,κ)f^{-1}(\varepsilon,\kappa), with ε=ε1+i​ε2\varepsilon=\varepsilon_{1}+i\varepsilon_{2}. Both these fibrations near 0 are locally isomorphic to the fibration induced by ℱ^=(H^+,H^−,L^)\widehat{\mathcal{F}}=(\widehat{H}_{+},\widehat{H}_{-},\widehat{L}) on 𝐒∗,u2×𝐒∗,v2×[−2,∞)\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{*,v}\times[-2,\infty) in a neighborhood of c∗c_{*}. ∎

We obtain as a corollary the following proposition. We emphasize that c∗c_{*} is a degenerate singularity and thus is not included in the classification given by Eliasson [18].

Proposition 16.

The reduced fiber F^−1​(𝔠∗)=ℱ^−1​(0)\widehat{F}^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*})=\widehat{\mathcal{F}}^{-1}(0) is topologically a sphere 𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2} with one singular point c∗c_{*}, which is an A2A_{2} singularity.

Proof.

By Theorem 15, we have that for a small ball B′B^{\prime} around the singularity c∗c_{*} and for (h1,h2,k)(h_{1},h_{2},k) sufficiently close to the critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}, the topology of F^−1​(h1,h2,k)∩B′\widehat{F}^{-1}(h_{1},h_{2},k)\cap B^{\prime} coincides with the one of ff. Therefore, it suffices to analyze the topology of the fibers of ff.

For κ=0\kappa=0, the singularity x=y=0x=y=0 of ff is the standard A2A_{2} singularity, and f−1​(0)f^{-1}(0) is a Riemann surface which is homeomorphic to 𝐂\mathbf{C}. In particular, if we consider a small ball B={|x|2+|y|2≤ρ}⊆𝐂2B=\{|x|^{2}+|y|^{2}\leq\rho\}\subseteq\mathbf{C}^{2}, then f−1​(0)∩Bf^{-1}(0)\cap B is homeomorphic to a two-dimensional disk which is singular at the origin.

Moreover, F^−1​(h1,h2,k)\widehat{F}^{-1}(h_{1},h_{2},k) for (h1,h2,k)(h_{1},h_{2},k) sufficiently close to, and including, the central critical value 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}, is a fibration outside B′B^{\prime} and, therefore, F^−1​(𝔠∗)∖B′\widehat{F}^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*})\setminus B^{\prime} is also an open disk. Therefore, the singular fiber F^−1​(𝔠∗)\widehat{F}^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*}) is the union of two disks, the disk F^−1​(𝔠∗)∩B′\widehat{F}^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*})\cap B^{\prime} containing the singular point c∗c_{*}, and the disk F^−1​(𝔠∗)∖B′\widehat{F}^{-1}(\mathfrak{c}_{*})\setminus B^{\prime}. The two disks are glued along a common boundary on B′B^{\prime} to form a topological 𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2} with one singular point c∗c_{*}. ∎

Remark 17.

We can further develop the analysis in the proof of Theorem 15 to recover the topology of the fibers corresponding to values near 𝔠∗\mathfrak{c}_{*}. For κ=0\kappa=0, |ε||\varepsilon| sufficiently small and ε≠0\varepsilon\neq 0, f−1​(ε)∩Bf^{-1}(\varepsilon)\cap B is a two-dimensional torus from which an open disk has been deleted; as well as f−1​(0)∩Bf^{-1}(0)\cap B for κ≠0\kappa\neq 0. For κ≠0\kappa\neq 0 and ε≠0\varepsilon\neq 0, two cases arise. The topology of f−1​(ε)∩Bf^{-1}(\varepsilon)\cap B is a two-dimensional torus with an open disk removed when the polynomial x3+κ​x−εx^{3}+\kappa x-\varepsilon has three simple roots, and a two-dimensional pinched torus with an open disk removed when the polynomial has a double root. The latter case occurs for (ε,κ)(\varepsilon,\kappa) on the discriminant locus Δ\Delta of the polynomial.

Remark 18.

We note here that our use of the term “A2A_{2} singularity” follows the classification of singularities in, e.g., [2] and differs from the terminology in [28] where “A2A_{2}” corresponds to a cuspidal singularity. In both cases, the A2A_{2} singularity corresponds to the equation x2+y3=0x^{2}+y^{3}=0, but x,y∈𝐂x,y\in\mathbf{C} in this work, while x,y∈𝐑x,y\in\mathbf{R} in [28].

6.2 Picard-Lefschetz monodromy

In the previous section, we showed that the local fibration around the central singularity induced by the reduced system is locally topologically equivalent to the local fibration around 0 given by the function f:𝐂2×𝐑→𝐂×𝐑f:\mathbf{C}^{2}\times\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{C}\times\mathbf{R} with

f​(x,y,κ)=(y2+x3+κ​x,κ),f(x,y,\kappa)=(y^{2}+x^{3}+\kappa x,\kappa), (17)

and with fibers f−1​(ε,κ)f^{-1}(\varepsilon,\kappa) given by

y2+x3+κ​x=ε.y^{2}+x^{3}+\kappa x=\varepsilon. (18)

Equation (18) defines a family of Riemann surfaces, Rε,κR_{\varepsilon,\kappa}, with ε∈𝐂\varepsilon\in\mathbf{C} and κ∈𝐑\kappa\in\mathbf{R}.

As explained in the previous section, these Riemann surfaces are singular for the values of (ε,κ)∈𝐂×𝐑(\varepsilon,\kappa)\in\mathbf{C}\times\mathbf{R} on the discriminant locus Δ\Delta of the polynomial

x3+κ​x−ε,x^{3}+\kappa x-\varepsilon, (19)

given by the equation

ε2=−427​κ3.\varepsilon^{2}=-\frac{4}{27}\kappa^{3}.

The discriminant locus Δ\Delta of the polynomial x3+κ​x−εx^{3}+\kappa x-\varepsilon consists of four threads, λj\lambda_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4, see Figure 3. The threads λ1\lambda_{1} and λ2\lambda_{2} form a cusp at 0 and are respectively parameterized as ε=−i​(4​κ3/27)1/2\varepsilon=-i(4\kappa^{3}/27)^{1/2} and ε=i​(4​κ3/27)1/2\varepsilon=i(4\kappa^{3}/27)^{1/2} with κ>0\kappa>0. Since ε∈i​𝐑\varepsilon\in i\mathbf{R} on λ1,2\lambda_{1,2} we refer to them as imaginary threads. The real threads λ3\lambda_{3} and λ4\lambda_{4} are respectively parameterized as ε=(4​|κ|3/27)1/2\varepsilon=(4|\kappa|^{3}/27)^{1/2} and ε=−(4​|κ|3/27)1/2\varepsilon=-(4|\kappa|^{3}/27)^{1/2} with κ<0\kappa<0, and similarly form a cusp at 0.

The map ϕ:𝐑3→𝐂×𝐑\phi:\mathbf{R}^{3}\to\mathbf{C}\times\mathbf{R} in Theorem 15 sends the set of critical values of ℱ\mathcal{F} in a neighborhood of 0 to the discriminant locus Δ\Delta of the polynomial (19) in a neighborhood of 0. In particular, it maps each thread ℓj\ell_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4, to the thread λj\lambda_{j} of Δ\Delta. Figure 3(a) shows the set of critical values near 0 of the integral map ℱ\mathcal{F}, and the discriminant locus Δ\Delta in the space (ε1,ε2,κ)(\varepsilon_{1},\varepsilon_{2},\kappa) near 0.

We now proceed to compute the Picard-Lefschetz monodromy of the fibration given by the Riemann surfaces Rε,κR_{\varepsilon,\kappa} over the base space 𝐂×𝐑\mathbf{C}\times\mathbf{R}. We fix a base point (ε,κ)=(1,0)(\varepsilon,\kappa)=(1,0) in the base space. Then the fundamental group π1​(Δc,(1,0))\pi_{1}(\Delta^{c},(1,0)) of the complement of Δ\Delta with base point (1,0)(1,0), acts on the first homology group of the fibers Rε,κR_{\varepsilon,\kappa}, (ε,κ)∉Δ(\varepsilon,\kappa)\not\in\Delta. The action N​(a)N(a) of the fundamental group on a cycle aa is determined via the Picard-Lefschetz formula [39]

N​(a)=a−(a,δ)​δ,N(a)=a-(a,\delta)\delta, (20)

where δ\delta is a vanishing cycle.

The Riemann surfaces Rε,κR_{\varepsilon,\kappa}, for (ε,κ)∉Δ(\varepsilon,\kappa)\not\in\Delta, are given by a two-sheet covering (two values of yy), with three ramification points given by the roots of (19). The first homology group of the fibers Rε,κR_{\varepsilon,\kappa} is two-dimensional and its bases and monodromy can be analyzed using the roots of (19). For κ=0\kappa=0, ε=1\varepsilon=1, let xj=e2​π​i​j/3x_{j}=e^{2\pi ij/3}, j=0,1,2j=0,1,2, be the corresponding roots of (19). Let αj\alpha_{j}, j=0,1,2j=0,1,2 be the cycles in R0,1R_{0,1}, obtained by lifting the segment from xj−1x_{j-1} to xjx_{j} in the upper sheet and then backward from xjx_{j} to xj−1x_{j-1} in the lower sheet. Here and in the sequel, we use the convention that x−1=x2x_{-1}=x_{2} and α−1=α2\alpha_{-1}=\alpha_{2}. The branching points of R1,0R_{1,0} and the segments between them that give the cycles αj\alpha_{j} are represented in Figure 4. Denoting by (αi,αi+1)(\alpha_{i},\alpha_{i+1}) the intersection number of the cycles αi\alpha_{i}, αi+1\alpha_{i+1}, we have

α0+α1+α2=0,(αi,αi+1)=1.\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}=0,\quad(\alpha_{i},\alpha_{i+1})=1. (21)
