To express monodromy as a matrix we work with the basis {α1,α0}\{\alpha_{1},\alpha_{0}\}. If the monodromy transformation along a path γ\gamma is N​(α1)=n11​α1+n12​α0N(\alpha_{1})=n_{11}\alpha_{1}+n_{12}\alpha_{0}, N​(α0)=n21​α1+n22​α0N(\alpha_{0})=n_{21}\alpha_{1}+n_{22}\alpha_{0}, then we express the transformation as the matrix

N​(γ)=[n11n12n21n22].N(\gamma)=\begin{bmatrix}n_{11}&n_{12}\\ n_{21}&n_{22}\end{bmatrix}.

Similarly to the discussion in Section 5.2, Proposition 13, we have π1​(Δc,(1,0))≃𝐙∗𝐙∗𝐙\pi_{1}(\Delta^{c},(1,0))\simeq\mathbf{Z}*\mathbf{Z}*\mathbf{Z}. We compute Picard-Lefschetz monodromy along representatives γ^j\hat{\gamma}_{j}, j=1,…,4j=1,\dots,4, of homotopy classes, such that each γ^j\hat{\gamma}_{j} winds once around the thread λj\lambda_{j} and is homotopically equivalent to the path ψ∘γj\psi\circ\gamma_{j}, where γj\gamma_{j} is the path used to compute Hamiltonian monodromy in Section 5.2.

We start by analyzing the monodromy around the real threads λ3\lambda_{3}, λ4\lambda_{4}. Let γ^3\hat{\gamma}_{3} be the path starting at (ε,κ)=(1,0)(\varepsilon,\kappa)=(1,0), decreasing the value of κ\kappa until it arrives close to λ3\lambda_{3}, then positively winding in the complex ε\varepsilon-plane and back to the starting point, see Figure 3(b).

For real parameters ε\varepsilon, κ\kappa, inside the cusp region there are three real roots of (19) and outside only one. This means that along this first path (19) has one real and two complex conjugate roots. The complex conjugate roots are obtained by transporting the roots x1x_{1} and x2x_{2}, while the root x0x_{0} is not affected. The complex conjugate roots coalesce when touching the cusp curve implying that the cycle α2=−α0−α1\alpha_{2}=-\alpha_{0}-\alpha_{1} is the vanishing cycle along this path. Hence, using the Picard-Lefschetz formula, Eq. (20), and the intersection numbers, Eq. (21), we obtain N3​(α0)=α0+α2=−α1N_{3}(\alpha_{0})=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}=-\alpha_{1}, and N3​(α1)=α1−α2=α0+2​α1N_{3}(\alpha_{1})=\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}=\alpha_{0}+2\alpha_{1}.

The general approach for dealing with the monodromy around the other threads is similar, but we must first transport the roots xjx_{j} (and hence the cycles αj\alpha_{j}) around a convenient path, as shown in Figure 3(b).

To calculate the monodromy transformation N4N_{4} along a path γ^4\hat{\gamma}_{4} around the real thread λ4\lambda_{4}, we first consider a half-circle (in the negative direction) in the complex ε\varepsilon-plane, κ=0\kappa=0. It affects the roots xjx_{j} by rotation by −π/3-\pi/3, that is, the roots are transformed to xi′=xi​e−π​i/3x^{\prime}_{i}=x_{i}e^{-\pi i/3} and the cycles αi\alpha_{i} are transformed to αi′\alpha^{\prime}_{i} from xi−1′x^{\prime}_{i-1} to xi′x^{\prime}_{i}. We then decrease κ\kappa until arriving close to the lower cusp thread in the real (ε,κ)(\varepsilon,\kappa)-plane, then winding positively in the complex ε\varepsilon-plane and back to the starting point. The vanishing cycle is now α1′\alpha^{\prime}_{1}. Then, the Picard-Lefschetz formula, Eq. (20), and the intersection numbers, Eq. (21), give that N4​(α0)=α0−α1N_{4}(\alpha_{0})=\alpha_{0}-\alpha_{1} and N4​(α2)=α2+α1N_{4}(\alpha_{2})=\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{1}. Since α1=−α0−α2\alpha_{1}=-\alpha_{0}-\alpha_{2}, we obtain N4​(α1)=α1N_{4}(\alpha_{1})=\alpha_{1}.

We then consider the monodromy along a path γ^2\hat{\gamma}_{2} around the imaginary thread λ2\lambda_{2}. To obtain the purely imaginary part of ε\varepsilon, we have to wind by π/2\pi/2 in the complex ε\varepsilon-plane, for κ=0\kappa=0. We have

x′3+κ​x′=i​ε.{x^{\prime}}^{3}+\kappa x^{\prime}=i\varepsilon. (22)

Hence, the roots xjx_{j} are modified to xj′=xj​eπ​i/6x^{\prime}_{j}=x_{j}e^{\pi i/6}. We want to obtain a purely real equation. Substituting x′=−i​ξx^{\prime}=-i\xi, in (22) and dividing by ii, we get ξ3−κ​ξ=ε\xi^{3}-\kappa\xi=\varepsilon. The roots ξj\xi_{j}, obtained from xjx_{j}, j=0,1,2j=0,1,2, are ξj=xj+1\xi_{j}=x_{j+1} and the cycles αj′\alpha^{\prime}_{j} as previously go from ξj−1{\xi}_{j-1} to ξj{\xi}_{j}. We then increase κ\kappa until arriving close to the upper cusp thread in the plane i​𝐑×𝐑i\mathbf{R}\times\mathbf{R}, then winding positively in the complex ε\varepsilon-plane and back to the starting point. The vanishing cycle is α1′\alpha^{\prime}_{1}. This gives N2​(α1)=α1N_{2}(\alpha_{1})=\alpha_{1} and N2​(α0)=α0−α1N_{2}(\alpha_{0})=\alpha_{0}-\alpha_{1}.

We repeat the same procedure for λ1\lambda_{1} considering a path γ^1\hat{\gamma}_{1}. We first wind by −π/2-\pi/2 in ε\varepsilon. Thus, the roots xjx_{j} wind by −π/6-\pi/6, and we get the roots xj′=xj​e−π​i/6x^{\prime}_{j}=x_{j}e^{-\pi i/6}. We make the same change of variable as in the previous case, and dividing by ii, we get ξ3−κ​ξ=−ε\xi^{3}-\kappa\xi=-\varepsilon, obtaining that x0x_{0} is transformed to ξ0=eπ​i/3\xi_{0}=e^{\pi i/3}, ξ1=−1\xi_{1}=-1, and ξ2=e−π​i/3\xi_{2}=e^{-\pi i/3}. We get that the cycle α0′\alpha^{\prime}_{0} joining ξ2\xi_{2} to ξ0\xi_{0} is the vanishing cycle when we reach the imaginary thread λ1\lambda_{1}. This gives the monodromy N1​(α0)=α0N_{1}(\alpha_{0})=\alpha_{0}, N1​(α1)=α1+α0N_{1}(\alpha_{1})=\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{0}.

We finally summarize the results in the following proposition using the conventions described above.

Proposition 19.

The generators of the Picard-Lefschetz monodromy of the reduced Tavis-Cummings system in a neighborhood of the central A2A_{2} singularity are given by:

N​(γ^1)=[1101],N​(γ^2)=N​(γ^4)=[10−11],N​(γ^3)=[21−10].\displaystyle N(\hat{\gamma}_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix},\ N(\hat{\gamma}_{2})=N(\hat{\gamma}_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ -1&1\end{bmatrix},\ N(\hat{\gamma}_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\ -1&0\end{bmatrix}.

We stress that we obtain the lower right 2×22\times 2 block of the matrices obtained in Section 5.2. Note that in the full space, there is a global 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action. Hence, the additional cycle (given by the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action) in the full space is not affected by the monodromy.

7 Conclusion and perspectives

In this work, we investigated the topological structure of the singular Lagrangian fibration of a special case of the two-spin Tavis–Cummings (TC) system. For the specific choice of parameters defining the special Tavis-Cummings system, we have identified what is, to the best of our knowledge, the first physical example of a completely integrable Hamiltonian system exhibiting an A2A_{2} singularity. Moreover, we described the topology of all singular fibers in the interior of the image of the integral map and showed that the Lagrangian fibration by three-dimensional tori over the regular values of the integral map is not globally trivializable by explicitly computing the monodromy matrices of the system. We further demonstrated the correspondence between the Hamiltonian monodromy of the system and the Picard-Lefschetz monodromy of the versal unfolding of the A2A_{2} singularity. We conclude this paper with some additional perspectives on the Lax pair formalism and the appearance of the A2A_{2} singularity in the STC system.

A distinctive feature of the TC system is that, in any number of degrees of freedom, it admits a description in terms of a spectral Lax pair. As discussed in Appendix D, it is precisely through a Lax pair approach that we have identified the parameter values corresponding to the special TC system. In a forthcoming paper, we will study how to recover the bifurcation diagram and analyze Hamiltonian monodromy using the spectral Lax pair of the system, as this algebraic approach appears to provide a systematic method for addressing these questions [4, 7, 23].

The fine-tuning of the parameters required for the appearance of the A2A_{2} singularity raises the question of how the system changes under small parameter variations. Recall that to define the STC system we chose specific values of the parameters δ1=1/2\delta_{1}=1/2, δ2=3/2\delta_{2}=3/2, ω=1\omega=1, satisfying the resonance condition δ1+δ2=2​ω\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}=2\omega. Fixing parameter values allowed us to draw the sets of critical values in Figure 1 and to numerically compute the Hamiltonian monodromy of the system. However, the proof of Proposition 7 and the discussion in Appendix D, show that the TC system has an A2A_{2} singularity for a wide range of parameters δ1,δ2>0\delta_{1},\delta_{2}>0 satisfying the resonance condition δ1+δ2=2​ω\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}=2\omega with δ1≠δ2\delta_{1}\neq\delta_{2}. If the resonance condition is not satisfied, then the system does not have an A2A_{2} singularity. We have checked that if we keep δ1=1/2\delta_{1}=1/2, δ2=3/2\delta_{2}=3/2 fixed, and detune ω\omega, then the four threads ℓ1,…,ℓ4\ell_{1},\dots,\ell_{4} do not meet at a central critical value. For ω\omega slightly larger than 11, the threads ℓ1\ell_{1} and ℓ4\ell_{4} combine to form a smooth thread of rank-11 non-degenerate focus-focus-regular singularities, while ℓ2\ell_{2} similarly combines with ℓ3\ell_{3}. The new combined threads do not intersect. For ω\omega slightly smaller than 11, ℓ1\ell_{1} combines with ℓ3\ell_{3}, while ℓ2\ell_{2} combines with ℓ4\ell_{4}. That is, a small detuning of the resonance condition replaces the four threads meeting at the A2A_{2} singularity with two smooth non-intersecting threads.

Recall that the Jaynes-Cummings (JC) system, that is, the TC system with N=1N=1 spins, has an A1A_{1} (focus-focus) singularity, which is associated with non-trivial Hamiltonian monodromy. It is then natural to ask if higher-order ANA_{N} singularities with N≥2N\geq 2 can also appear in integrable Hamiltonian systems with compact singular Lagrangian fibrations, and what are their implications. In this paper, we answered these questions for the case N=2N=2, as we showed that the A2A_{2} singularity appears in the TC system with N=2N=2 spins.

We note that there is no general method for constructing an integrable Hamiltonian system with an A2A_{2} singularity having a compact singular Lagrangian fibration given by an explicit integral map. An attempt to construct such a system by applying Flaschka’s method to the holomorphic function f:𝐂2→𝐂f:\mathbf{C}^{2}\to\mathbf{C} [20] with f​(x,y)=y2+x3f(x,y)=y^{2}+x^{3}, corresponding to the A2A_{2} singularity, produces a 2-DOF integrable Hamiltonian system with non-complete flows and non-compact fibers. In particular, the regular fibers are two-dimensional tori with a disk removed—these are the Riemann surfaces described in Section 6.2. Therefore, the STC system provides a compactification of this system in an algebraically closed form for the integral map, while at the same time being physically significant.

The question of the existence of integrable Hamiltonian systems with an ANA_{N} singularity having a compact singular Lagrangian fibration given by an explicit integral map remains open for N≥3N\geq 3. We conjecture that the TC system with NN spins may provide such examples, based on the observation that the existence of the A1A_{1} singularity in the JC system and of the A2A_{2} singularity in the two-spin TC system is related to the spectral Lax pair formalism and the degeneracy of the roots of the spectral curve. Recall that for the TC system with NN spins, the Lax pair approach [5, 23], associates to the TC system a complex polynomial Q2​N+2​(λ)Q_{2N+2}(\lambda) of degree 2​N+22N+2, with real coefficients. The spectral curve is the Riemann surface defined by μ2=Q2​N+2​(λ)\mu^{2}=Q_{2N+2}(\lambda). In the case N=1N=1, the polynomial Q4​(λ)Q_{4}(\lambda) has, for specific choices of parameters, two complex conjugate roots with multiplicity 22 which correspond to the A1A_{1} singularity of the JC system. Similarly, in the case N=2N=2, the polynomial Q6​(λ)Q_{6}(\lambda) has, for specific choices of parameters, two complex conjugate roots with multiplicity 33 which, as we showed in this paper, correspond to an A2A_{2} singularity. In general, for NN spins we may expect that the polynomial Q2​N+2​(λ)Q_{2N+2}(\lambda) will have for specific choices of parameters two complex conjugate roots λ∗\lambda_{*}, λ¯∗\bar{\lambda}_{*} with multiplicity N+1N+1. That is, the corresponding Riemann surface μ2=Q2​N+2​(λ)\mu^{2}=Q_{2N+2}(\lambda) will have an ANA_{N} singularity at λ∗\lambda_{*}, since the local normal form around this singularity is μ2=λN+1\mu^{2}=\lambda^{N+1}, and, for the same reason, an ANA_{N} singularity at λ¯∗\bar{\lambda}_{*}. This raises the possibility that the corresponding integral map also has a highly degenerate ANA_{N} singularity. We would call a TC system with NN spins having this property a special Tavis-Cummings system of order NN (STCN system). Based on these observations, we formulate the following conjecture, generalizing what is known for N=1,2N=1,2:

Conjecture.

For each N≥1N\geq 1, there exists a special Tavis-Cummings system of order NN, that is, there exist parameter configurations such that the polynomial Q2​N+2​(λ)Q_{2N+2}(\lambda) for the TC system with NN spins has two complex conjugate roots with multiplicity N+1N+1, and for these parameter configurations the integral map has a highly degenerate ANA_{N} singularity.

Acknowledgments

The second, third, and fourth author have been supported by the EIPHI Graduate school (contract “ANR-17-EURE-0002”) and by the Région Bourgogne Franche-Comté. The third author also benefited from the support of the IRL 2001 Solomon Lefschetz CNRS-UNAM, Croatian Science Foundation (HRZZ) grant IP-2022-10-9820, partial support by the Horizon grant 101183111-DSYREKI-HORIZON-MSCA-2023-SE-01.

Appendix A Reduction of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action

In this section we describe in detail the reduction of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action φK\varphi_{K} (1) in terms of algebraic invariants. The 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action has the linear invariants u3u_{3}, v3v_{3} and the quadratic invariants |z|2|z|^{2}, |u|2=1−u32|u|^{2}=1-u_{3}^{2}, |v|2=1−v32|v|^{2}=1-v_{3}^{2}, u​v¯u\bar{v}, u​z¯u\bar{z}, and v​z¯v\bar{z}. All polynomial invariants of φK\varphi_{K} factor through the given linear and quadratic invariants. This can be shown by considering the diagonal action of φK\varphi_{K} on the space of monomials in variables u3,v3,u,v,zu_{3},v_{3},u,v,z, given by

u3n1​v3n2​un3​u¯n4​un5​u¯n6​zn7​z¯n8→φKtei​(n3+n5+n7−n4−n6−n8)​t​u3n1​v3n2​un3​u¯n4​vn5​v¯n6​zn7​z¯n8.u_{3}^{n_{1}}v_{3}^{n_{2}}u^{n_{3}}\bar{u}^{n_{4}}u^{n_{5}}\bar{u}^{n_{6}}z^{n_{7}}\bar{z}^{n_{8}}\xrightarrow{\ \varphi_{K}^{t}\ }e^{i(n_{3}+n_{5}+n_{7}-n_{4}-n_{6}-n_{8})t}u_{3}^{n_{1}}v_{3}^{n_{2}}u^{n_{3}}\bar{u}^{n_{4}}v^{n_{5}}\bar{v}^{n_{6}}z^{n_{7}}\bar{z}^{n_{8}}.

For a monomial to be invariant under the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action we must have n3+n5+n7=n4+n6+n8n_{3}+n_{5}+n_{7}=n_{4}+n_{6}+n_{8} and, therefore, each of the factors u,v,zu,v,z must be paired with exactly one of u¯,v¯,z¯\bar{u},\bar{v},\bar{z}. Then, a theorem of Schwarz ensures that smooth functions invariant under the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action also factor through the same invariants [33].

For the algebraic reduction we use here the following real invariants which are combinations of the linear and quadratic invariants given earlier:

u3,v3,K,X1+i​Y1=v​z¯,X2+i​Y2=z​u¯,X3+i​Y3=u​v¯.u_{3},\ v_{3},\ K,\ X_{1}+iY_{1}=v\bar{z},\ X_{2}+iY_{2}=z\bar{u},\ X_{3}+iY_{3}=u\bar{v}. (23)

Expressed in terms of real coordinates, we have

X1\displaystyle X_{1} =p​v1+q​v2,\displaystyle=pv_{1}+qv_{2}, X2\displaystyle X_{2} =p​u1+q​u2,\displaystyle=pu_{1}+qu_{2}, X3\displaystyle X_{3} =u1​v1+u2​v2,\displaystyle=u_{1}v_{1}+u_{2}v_{2},
Y1\displaystyle Y_{1} =−q​v1+p​v2,\displaystyle=-qv_{1}+pv_{2}, Y2\displaystyle Y_{2} =q​u1−p​u2,\displaystyle=qu_{1}-pu_{2}, Y3\displaystyle Y_{3} =u2​v1−u1​v2.\displaystyle=u_{2}v_{1}-u_{1}v_{2}.

The dynamics in the reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is then determined by the Poisson structure given in Table 1.

The algebraic invariants in Eq. (23) are not independent. They satisfy the syzygies σk=0\sigma_{k}=0, k=1,…,9k=1,\dots,9, where

σ1\displaystyle\sigma_{1} =X12+Y12−2​(1−v32)​(K−u3−v3),\displaystyle=X_{1}^{2}+Y_{1}^{2}-2(1-v_{3}^{2})(K-u_{3}-v_{3}),
σ2\displaystyle\sigma_{2} =X22+Y22−2​(1−u32)​(K−u3−v3),\displaystyle=X_{2}^{2}+Y_{2}^{2}-2(1-u_{3}^{2})(K-u_{3}-v_{3}),
σ3\displaystyle\sigma_{3} =X32+Y32−(1−v32)​(1−u32),\displaystyle=X_{3}^{2}+Y_{3}^{2}-(1-v_{3}^{2})(1-u_{3}^{2}),
σ4\displaystyle\sigma_{4} =X1​X3−Y1​Y3−(1−v32)​X2,\displaystyle=X_{1}X_{3}-Y_{1}Y_{3}-(1-v_{3}^{2})X_{2},
σ5\displaystyle\sigma_{5} =X1​Y3+X3​Y1+(1−v32)​Y2,\displaystyle=X_{1}Y_{3}+X_{3}Y_{1}+(1-v_{3}^{2})Y_{2},
σ6\displaystyle\sigma_{6} =X2​X3−Y2​Y3−(1−u32)​X1,\displaystyle=X_{2}X_{3}-Y_{2}Y_{3}-(1-u_{3}^{2})X_{1},
σ7\displaystyle\sigma_{7} =X2​Y3+X3​Y2+(1−u32)​Y1,\displaystyle=X_{2}Y_{3}+X_{3}Y_{2}+(1-u_{3}^{2})Y_{1},
σ8\displaystyle\sigma_{8} =X1​X2−Y1​Y2−2​(K−u3−v3)​X3,\displaystyle=X_{1}X_{2}-Y_{1}Y_{2}-2(K-u_{3}-v_{3})X_{3},
σ9\displaystyle\sigma_{9} =X1​Y2+X2​Y1+2​(K−u3−v3)​Y3.\displaystyle=X_{1}Y_{2}+X_{2}Y_{1}+2(K-u_{3}-v_{3})Y_{3}.

Notice that the given syzygies are themselves not independent.

Finally, we note that the functions H1H_{1} and H2H_{2} can be expressed in terms of the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} algebraic invariants as

H^1\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{1} =(δ1−ω)​u3+2​g​Y2−2​g2δ2−δ1​(X3+u3​v3),\displaystyle=(\delta_{1}-\omega)u_{3}+\sqrt{2}gY_{2}-\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(X_{3}+u_{3}v_{3}),
H^2\displaystyle\widehat{H}_{2} =(δ2−ω)​v3−2​g​Y1+2​g2δ2−δ1​(X3+u3​v3).\displaystyle=(\delta_{2}-\omega)v_{3}-\sqrt{2}gY_{1}+\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(X_{3}+u_{3}v_{3}).

Recall that the reduced spaces K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} are smooth manifolds for k∈(−2,0)∪(0,2)∪(2,∞)k\in(-2,0)\cup(0,2)\cup(2,\infty). We have the following characterization.

Proposition 20.

The reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is diffeomorphic to 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2}, for k∈(−2,0)k\in(-2,0). It is diffeomorphic to a symplectic blow-up of 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2} on two disjoint balls, or—equivalently—the symplectic blow-up of 𝐒2×𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{2} on a ball, for k∈(0,2)k\in(0,2). Finally, it is diffeomorphic to 𝐒2×𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}, for k∈(2,∞)k\in(2,\infty).

Proof.

To prove this statement we exhibit the manifolds K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} as compact toric manifolds and we compute the corresponding Delzant polytopes [13]. In particular, consider the Hamiltonian 𝐓3\mathbf{T}^{3} action Ψ\Psi on MM given by

Ψ​((ei​t,ei​s1,ei​s2),(u,u3;v,v3;z))=φKt∘φJ1s1∘φJ2s2​(u,u3;v,v3;z)=(ei​(t+s1)​u,u3;ei​(t+s2)​v,v3;ei​t​z),\Psi((e^{it},e^{is_{1}},e^{is_{2}}),(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z))=\varphi_{K}^{t}\circ\varphi_{J_{1}}^{s_{1}}\circ\varphi_{J_{2}}^{s_{2}}(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z)=(e^{i(t+s_{1})}{\color[rgb]{1,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{1,0,0}u},u_{3};e^{i(t+s_{2})}v,v_{3};e^{it}z),

where J1=u3J_{1}=u_{3}, J2=v3J_{2}=v_{3}. The action Ψ\Psi induces an effective 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action ψ\psi on the reduced space K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}. In terms of algebraic invariants |z|2,u3,v3,u​v¯,u​z¯,v​z¯|z|^{2},u_{3},v_{3},u\bar{v},u\bar{z},v\bar{z}, the 𝐓2\mathbf{T}^{2} action ψ\psi is expressed as

ψ​((ei​s1,ei​s2),(|z|2,u3,v3,u​v¯,u​z¯,v​z¯))=(|z|2,u3,v3,ei​(s1−s2)​u​v¯,ei​s1​u​z¯,ei​s2​v​z¯).\psi((e^{is_{1}},e^{is_{2}}),(|z|^{2},u_{3},v_{3},u\bar{v},u\bar{z},v\bar{z}))=(|z|^{2},u_{3},v_{3},e^{i(s_{1}-s_{2})}u\bar{v},e^{is_{1}}u\bar{z},e^{is_{2}}v\bar{z}). (24)

The Delzant polytope DkD_{k} for the action ψ\psi on K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} can be determined as the convex hull of its vertices; the latter correspond to fixed points of ψ\psi. The expression in Eq. (24) implies that the fixed points of ψ\psi satisfy the relations u​v¯=0u\bar{v}=0, u​z¯=0u\bar{z}=0, and v​z¯=0v\bar{z}=0. Therefore, we can distinguish the following cases.

  1. (i)

    If u=v=0u=v=0 then we find J1=u3=±1J_{1}=u_{3}=\pm 1 and J2=v3=±1J_{2}=v_{3}=\pm 1. Since on K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k) we have |z|2=2​(k−u3−v3)|z|^{2}=2(k-u_{3}-v_{3}) we must have k≥u3+v3k\geq u_{3}+v_{3}. Therefore, the vertex (J1,J2)=(−1,−1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(-1,-1) can be attained for all k≥−2k\geq-2, while the vertices (J1,J2)=(−1,1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(-1,1) and (J1,J2)=(1,−1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(1,-1) can be attained, for all k≥0k\geq 0, and the vertex (J1,J2)=(1,1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(1,1) can be attained, for all k≥2k\geq 2.

  2. (ii)

    If u=z=0u=z=0, then J1=u3=±1J_{1}=u_{3}=\pm 1 and J2=k−J1=k∓1J_{2}=k-J_{1}=k\mp 1 with |J2|≤1|J_{2}|\leq 1. Therefore, we obtain the vertices (J1,J2)=(1,k−1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(1,k-1), for 0≤k≤20\leq k\leq 2 and (J1,J2)=(−1,k+1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(-1,k+1) for −2≤k≤0-2\leq k\leq 0.

  3. (iii)

    If v=z=0v=z=0, then J2=v3=±1J_{2}=v_{3}=\pm 1 and J1=k−J2=k∓1J_{1}=k-J_{2}=k\mp 1 with |J1|≤1|J_{1}|\leq 1. Therefore, we obtain the vertices (J1,J2)=(k−1,1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(k-1,1), for 0≤k≤20\leq k\leq 2 and (J1,J2)=(k+1,−1)(J_{1},J_{2})=(k+1,-1), for −2≤k≤0-2\leq k\leq 0.

To conclude, for −2≤k≤0-2\leq k\leq 0 the Delzant polygon DkD_{k} has three vertices, (k+1,−1)(k+1,-1), (−1,k+1)(-1,k+1), and (−1,−1)(-1,-1) and therefore in this case K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is diffeomorphic to 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2}. For k>2k>2 the Delzant polygon DkD_{k} has four vertices, (±1,±1)(\pm 1,\pm 1), and therefore in this case K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is diffeomorphic to 𝐒2×𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}. Finally, for the case 0≤k≤20\leq k\leq 2 of intermediate values the Delzant polygon DkD_{k} has five vertices, (k−1,1)(k-1,1), (1,k−1)(1,k-1), (1,−1)(1,-1), (−1,1)(-1,1), and (−1,−1)(-1,-1). This polygon can be obtained from the square [−1,1]2[-1,1]^{2} by cutting off a corner. This corresponds to a symplectic blowup of the space 𝐒2×𝐒2\mathbf{S}^{2}\times\mathbf{S}^{2} along a ball. Alternatively, the same polygon can be obtained from the triangle with vertices (k+1,−1)(k+1,-1), (−1,k+1)(-1,k+1), and (−1,−1)(-1,-1), by cutting off two corners. This corresponds to two symplectic blowups of the space 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2} along two disjoint balls. ∎

Notice that, the fact that K−1​(k)/𝐒1K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1} is diffeomorphic to 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2} when −2<k<0-2<k<0 also follows directly from the fact that KK is a definite positive function at (u,u3;v,v3;z)=(0,−1;0,−1;0)(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z)=(0,-1;0,-1;0). Therefore, each level set K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k) for kk greater than, but close to, −2-2 is diffeomorphic to 𝐒5\mathbf{S}^{5}. It can then be checked that reducing the given 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action on 𝐒5\mathbf{S}^{5} gives 𝐂𝐏2\mathbf{CP}^{2} as the reduced space.

Moreover, for k>2k>2 the map

𝐒u2×𝐒v2→K−1​(k)⊆𝐒u2×𝐒v2×𝐂∗:(u,u3;v,v3)↦(u,u3;v,v3;[2​(k−u3−v3)]1/2),\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\to K^{-1}(k)\subseteq\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\times\mathbf{C}_{*}\colon(u,u_{3};v,v_{3})\mapsto\bigl(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};[2(k-u_{3}-v_{3})]^{1/2}\bigr),

is a smooth global section for the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} bundle induced by the 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action on K−1​(k)K^{-1}(k). This follows from the fact that for points (u,u3;v,v3;z)∈K−1​(k)(u,u_{3};v,v_{3};z)\in K^{-1}(k), k>2k>2, we have |z|2=2​(k−u3−v3)>0|z|^{2}=2(k-u_{3}-v_{3})>0. This also shows that K−1​(k)≃𝐒u2×𝐒v2×𝐒1K^{-1}(k)\simeq\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}\times\mathbf{S}^{1} and thus it gives an explicit diffeomorphism K−1​(k)/𝐒1≃𝐒u2×𝐒v2K^{-1}(k)/\mathbf{S}^{1}\simeq\mathbf{S}^{2}_{u}\times\mathbf{S}^{2}_{v}.

Appendix B Rank-2 singularities

In this section we give the proof of Proposition 9 that describes the rank-22 singularities of the general TC system. We find rank-22 singular points by considering the relation XK=x​XH1+y​XH2X_{K}=xX_{H_{1}}+yX_{H_{2}} with x,y∈𝐑x,y\in\mathbf{R}.

First, the relation XK​(z)=x​XH1​(z)+y​XH2​(z)X_{K}(z)=xX_{H_{1}}(z)+yX_{H_{2}}(z) directly gives

z=2​i​g​(x​u+y​v).\displaystyle z=\sqrt{2}ig(xu+yv). (25)

Both relations 0=XK​(u3)=x​XH1​(u3)+y​XH2​(u3)0=X_{K}(u_{3})=xX_{H_{1}}(u_{3})+yX_{H_{2}}(u_{3}) and 0=XK​(v3)=x​XH1​(v3)+y​XH2​(v3)0=X_{K}(v_{3})=xX_{H_{1}}(v_{3})+yX_{H_{2}}(v_{3}) give after using (25) the relation

i​g2​[1δ2−δ1​(x−y)−x​y]​(u​v¯−u¯​v)=0.\displaystyle ig^{2}\Bigl[\frac{1}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(x-y)-xy\Bigr](u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v)=0.

Assuming u​v¯−u¯​v≠0u\bar{v}-\bar{u}v\neq 0 we find

x−y=(δ2−δ1)​x​y.\displaystyle x-y=(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})xy. (26)

Then, the relation XK​(u)=x​XH1​(u)+y​XH2​(u)X_{K}(u)=xX_{H_{1}}(u)+yX_{H_{2}}(u), after using (25), gives

[x​(δ1−ω)−2​g2​x2​u3−(x−y)​2​g2δ2−δ1​v3−1]​i​u+2​i​g2​[1δ2−δ1​(x−y)−x​y]​u3​v=0,\displaystyle\Bigl[x(\delta_{1}-\omega)-2g^{2}x^{2}u_{3}-(x-y)\frac{2g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}v_{3}-1\Bigr]iu+2ig^{2}\Bigl[\frac{1}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(x-y)-xy\Bigr]u_{3}v=0,

which, taking into account (26), simplifies to

2​g2​(x​u3+y​v3)=(δ1−ω)−1x.\displaystyle 2g^{2}(xu_{3}+yv_{3})=(\delta_{1}-\omega)-\frac{1}{x}.

Similarly, the relation XK​(v)=x​XH1​(v)+y​XH2​(v)X_{K}(v)=xX_{H_{1}}(v)+yX_{H_{2}}(v) gives

2​g2​(x​u3+y​v3)=(δ2−ω)−1y.\displaystyle 2g^{2}(xu_{3}+yv_{3})=(\delta_{2}-\omega)-\frac{1}{y}.

Let C=x​u3+y​v3C=xu_{3}+yv_{3}. Then, the previous two relations imply

C=x​u3+y​v3=12​g2​(δ1−ω)−12​g2​x=12​g2​(δ2−ω)−12​g2​y.\displaystyle C=xu_{3}+yv_{3}=\frac{1}{2g^{2}}(\delta_{1}-\omega)-\frac{1}{2g^{2}x}=\frac{1}{2g^{2}}(\delta_{2}-\omega)-\frac{1}{2g^{2}y}. (27)

Let U=u​v¯+u¯​vU=u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v. Then, using (25), we obtain

i​g2​(u​z¯−u¯​z)=g2​(2​x​u​u¯+y​(u​v¯+u¯​v))=2​x​g2​(1−u32)+y​g2​U,\displaystyle\frac{ig}{\sqrt{2}}(u\bar{z}-\bar{u}z)=g^{2}(2xu\bar{u}+y(u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v))=2xg^{2}(1-u_{3}^{2})+yg^{2}U,
i​g2​(v​z¯−v¯​z)=g2​(2​y​v​v¯+x​(u​v¯+u¯​v))=2​y​g2​(1−v32)+x​g2​U.\displaystyle\frac{ig}{\sqrt{2}}(v\bar{z}-\bar{v}z)=g^{2}(2yv\bar{v}+x(u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v))=2yg^{2}(1-v_{3}^{2})+xg^{2}U.

Substituting the previous two expressions into H1H_{1} in (4a) and H2H_{2} in (4b) we obtain.

H1\displaystyle H_{1} =(δ1−ω)​u3+2​x​g2​(1−u32)+y​g2​U−g2δ2−δ1​(U+2​u3​v3),\displaystyle=(\delta_{1}-\omega)u_{3}+2xg^{2}(1-u_{3}^{2})+yg^{2}U-\frac{g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(U+2u_{3}v_{3}), (28)
H2\displaystyle H_{2} =(δ2−ω)​v3+2​y​g2​(1−v32)+x​g2​U+g2δ2−δ1​(U+2​u3​v3).\displaystyle=(\delta_{2}-\omega)v_{3}+2yg^{2}(1-v_{3}^{2})+xg^{2}U+\frac{g^{2}}{\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}}(U+2u_{3}v_{3}). (29)

Moreover, using again (25), the momentum KK becomes

K\displaystyle K =u3+v3+12​z​z¯\displaystyle=u_{3}+v_{3}+\frac{1}{2}z\bar{z}
=u3+v3+g2​(x2​u​u¯+y2​v​v¯+x​y​(u​v¯+u¯​v))\displaystyle=u_{3}+v_{3}+g^{2}(x^{2}u\bar{u}+y^{2}v\bar{v}+xy(u\bar{v}+\bar{u}v))
=u3+v3+g2​x2​(1−u32)+g2​y2​(1−v32)+g2​x​y​U.\displaystyle=u_{3}+v_{3}+g^{2}x^{2}(1-u_{3}^{2})+g^{2}y^{2}(1-v_{3}^{2})+g^{2}xyU. (30)

Fixing the value of the momentum K=kK=k, we can solve (30) for UU, and substitute into (28) and (29). Moreover, using (27) to replace δ1−ω\delta_{1}-\omega and δ2−ω\delta_{2}-\omega, and taking into account (26), we obtain (16a) and (16b), that is,

(δ2−δ1)​H1\displaystyle(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})H_{1} =−kx2−C2​g2x2+Cx2​y+g2​(y2x2+2​xy−1),\displaystyle=-\frac{k}{x^{2}}-\frac{C^{2}g^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{C}{x^{2}y}+g^{2}\Bigl(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+2\frac{x}{y}-1\Bigr),
−(δ2−δ1)​H2\displaystyle-(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})H_{2} =−ky2−C2​g2y2+Cx​y2+g2​(x2y2+2​yx−1).\displaystyle=-\frac{k}{y^{2}}-\frac{C^{2}g^{2}}{y^{2}}+\frac{C}{xy^{2}}+g^{2}\Bigl(\frac{x^{2}}{y^{2}}+2\frac{y}{x}-1\Bigr).

We now want to determine bounds for the parameters xx, yy. Define

a=a1+i​a2=uz,b=b1+i​b2=vz.\displaystyle a=a_{1}+ia_{2}=\frac{u}{z},\ b=b_{1}+ib_{2}=\frac{v}{z}.

Then

|a|2=a12+a22=1−u322​(K−u3−v3),|b|2=b12+b22=1−v322​(K−u3−v3).\displaystyle|a|^{2}=a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}=\frac{1-u_{3}^{2}}{2(K-u_{3}-v_{3})},\ |b|^{2}=b_{1}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}=\frac{1-v_{3}^{2}}{2(K-u_{3}-v_{3})}.

Equation (25) gives 2​i​g​(x​a+y​b)=1\sqrt{2}ig(xa+yb)=1 from which we obtain

y2​|b|2=x2​|a|2−2​xg​a2+12​g2,\displaystyle y^{2}|b|^{2}=x^{2}|a|^{2}-\frac{\sqrt{2}x}{g}a_{2}+\frac{1}{2g^{2}},

and, by substituting |a|2|a|^{2}, |b|2|b|^{2} and using x​u3+y​v3=Cxu_{3}+yv_{3}=C, we arrive at

a2=g2​x​(12​g2+y2​C2−2​C​x​u3+x2−y2y​K−C+(x−y)​u3).\displaystyle a_{2}=\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}x}\Bigl(\frac{1}{2g^{2}}+\frac{y}{2}\frac{C^{2}-2Cxu_{3}+x^{2}-y^{2}}{yK-C+(x-y)u_{3}}\Bigr).

Then we have

a12=1−u322​(K−u3−v3)−a22=y2​1−u32y​K−C+(x−y)​u3−g22​x2​(12​g2+y2​C2−2​C​x​u3+x2−y2y​K−C+(x−y)​u3)2.\displaystyle a_{1}^{2}=\frac{1-u_{3}^{2}}{2(K-u_{3}-v_{3})}-a_{2}^{2}=\frac{y}{2}\frac{1-u_{3}^{2}}{yK-C+(x-y)u_{3}}-\frac{g^{2}}{2x^{2}}\Bigl(\frac{1}{2g^{2}}+\frac{y}{2}\frac{C^{2}-2Cxu_{3}+x^{2}-y^{2}}{yK-C+(x-y)u_{3}}\Bigr)^{2}.

We finally obtain

a12=18​g2​x2​(y​K−C+(x−y)​u3)2​P​(x,y;u3),\displaystyle a_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{8g^{2}x^{2}(yK-C+(x-y)u_{3})^{2}}P(x,y;u_{3}),

where P​(x,y;u3)P(x,y;u_{3}) is the following cubic polynomial in u3u_{3}:

P​(x,y;u3)\displaystyle P(x,y;u_{3}) =−4​g2​x2​y​(x−y)​u33\displaystyle=-4g^{2}x^{2}y(x-y)u_{3}^{3} (31)
+(−4​C2​g4​x2​y2+8​C​g2​x2​y−4​C​g2​x​y2−4​g2​K​x2​y2−(x−y)2)​u32\displaystyle+(-4C^{2}g^{4}x^{2}y^{2}+8Cg^{2}x^{2}y-4Cg^{2}xy^{2}-4g^{2}Kx^{2}y^{2}-(x-y)^{2})u_{3}^{2}
+2(2C3g4xy2−3C2g2xy+C2g2y2+2Cg4x3y2−2Cg4xy4+2Cg2Kxy2\displaystyle+2(2C^{3}g^{4}xy^{2}-3C^{2}g^{2}xy+C^{2}g^{2}y^{2}+2Cg^{4}x^{3}y^{2}-2Cg^{4}xy^{4}+2Cg^{2}Kxy^{2}
+Cx−Cy+g2x3y−g2x2y2+g2xy3−g2y4−Kxy+Ky2)u3\displaystyle\qquad+Cx-Cy+g^{2}x^{3}y-g^{2}x^{2}y^{2}+g^{2}xy^{3}-g^{2}y^{4}-Kxy+Ky^{2})u_{3}
−C4​g4​y2+2​C3​g2​y−2​C2​g4​x2​y2+2​C2​g4​y4−2​C2​g2​K​y2−C2−2​C​g2​x2​y\displaystyle-C^{4}g^{4}y^{2}+2C^{3}g^{2}y-2C^{2}g^{4}x^{2}y^{2}+2C^{2}g^{4}y^{4}-2C^{2}g^{2}Ky^{2}-C^{2}-2Cg^{2}x^{2}y
−2​C​g2​y3+2​C​K​y−g4​x4​y2+2​g4​x2​y4−g4​y6+2​g2​K​x2​y2+2​g2​K​y4−K2​y2.\displaystyle\qquad-2Cg^{2}y^{3}+2CKy-g^{4}x^{4}y^{2}+2g^{4}x^{2}y^{4}-g^{4}y^{6}+2g^{2}Kx^{2}y^{2}+2g^{2}Ky^{4}-K^{2}y^{2}.

We compute

P​(x,y;−1)\displaystyle P(x,y;-1) =−(C+x−y−(K+g2​(C+x)2)​y+g2​y3)2≤0,\displaystyle=-(C+x-y-(K+g^{2}(C+x)^{2})y+g^{2}y^{3})^{2}\leq 0,
P​(x,y;1)\displaystyle P(x,y;\phantom{-}1) =−(C−x+y−(K+g2​(C−x)2)​y+g2​y3)2≤0.\displaystyle=-(C-x+y-(K+g^{2}(C-x)^{2})y+g^{2}y^{3})^{2}\leq 0.

Therefore, P​(x,y;u3)P(x,y;u_{3}) either has 0 or 22 roots in [−1,1][-1,1]. Since a12≥0a_{1}^{2}\geq 0 and P​(x,y;±1)≤0P(x,y;\pm 1)\leq 0, the allowed values of x,yx,y are those for which P​(x,y;u3)P(x,y;u_{3}) has 22 roots in [−1,1][-1,1]. Since yy can be expressed in terms of xx as y=x/(1+x​(δ2−δ1))y=x/(1+x(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1})), we can determine those values of xx for which Q​(x;u3)=P​(x,x/(1+x​(δ2−δ1));u3)Q(x;u_{3})=P(x,x/(1+x(\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}));u_{3}) has 22 roots in [−1,1][-1,1]. These values form intervals and the boundaries of these intervals are determined by the conditions Q​(x;u3)=d​Q​(x;u3)/d​x=0Q(x;u_{3})=dQ(x;u_{3})/dx=0 with u3∈[−1,1]u_{3}\in[-1,1].

Appendix C Numerical computation of period lattices and Hamiltonian monodromy

In this section we provide details of the numerical computation of the period lattices that were used to determine Hamiltonian monodromy of the special Tavis-Cummings system in Section 5.2.

Recall that a vector 𝑻=(T1,T2,T3)∈𝐑3\boldsymbol{T}=(T_{1},T_{2},T_{3})\in\mathbf{R}^{3} is a period vector on a regular fiber F−1​(r)F^{-1}(r) if

φH1T1∘φH2T2∘φKT3|F−1​(r)=idF−1​(r).\varphi_{H_{1}}^{T_{1}}\circ\varphi_{H_{2}}^{T_{2}}\circ\varphi_{K}^{T_{3}}|_{F^{-1}(r)}=\mathrm{id}_{F^{-1}(r)}.

Since XKX_{K} generates an 𝐒1\mathbf{S}^{1} action with period 2​π2\pi, the constant vector 𝑻1=(0,0,2​π)\boldsymbol{T}_{1}=(0,0,2\pi) is a period vector on every regular value rr of FF. To compute a basis of the period lattice on F−1​(r)F^{-1}(r) we need to compute two more period vectors 𝑻2​(r)\boldsymbol{T}_{2}(r) and 𝑻3​(r)\boldsymbol{T}_{3}(r).

To compute period vectors we work as follows. Given a regular value r=(h1,h2,k)r=(h_{1},h_{2},k) of FF we first compute a point P∈F−1​(r)P\in F^{-1}(r) by numerically solving the equations H1​(P)=h1H_{1}(P)=h_{1}, H2​(P)=h2H_{2}(P)=h_{2}, K​(P)=kK(P)=k. Then, we consider the function m:𝐑3→F−1​(r)m:\mathbf{R}^{3}\to F^{-1}(r) given by

m​(𝑻)=φH1T1∘φH2T2∘φKT3​(P),m(\boldsymbol{T})=\varphi_{H_{1}}^{T_{1}}\circ\varphi_{H_{2}}^{T_{2}}\circ\varphi_{K}^{T_{3}}(P),

with fixed P∈F−1​(r)P\in F^{-1}(r) and we use the Newton-Raphson method to solve the three equations

θu​(m​(𝑻))−θu​(P)=0,θv​(m​(𝑻))−θv​(P)=0,ϕ​(m​(𝑻))−ϕ​(P)=0,\theta_{u}(m(\boldsymbol{T}))-\theta_{u}(P)=0,\ \theta_{v}(m(\boldsymbol{T}))-\theta_{v}(P)=0,\ \phi(m(\boldsymbol{T}))-\phi(P)=0,\

given an initial guess for the period vector 𝑻\boldsymbol{T}. Here, (ϕ,J)(\phi,J) are symplectic polar coordinates on 𝐑∗2\mathbf{R}^{2}_{*} defined by p+i​q=2​J​exp⁡(i​ϕ)p+iq=\sqrt{2J}\exp(i\phi).

First, we consider the regular value r0=(2,1,1.8)r_{0}=(2,1,1.8) of F=(H1,H2,K)F=(H_{1},H_{2},K). Then we compute that two period vectors which together with 𝑻1=(0,0,2​π)\boldsymbol{T}_{1}=(0,0,2\pi) form a basis of the period lattice at r0r_{0} are given by

𝑻2\displaystyle\boldsymbol{T}_{2} ≈(1.83862,2.07173,−1.44104),𝑻3\displaystyle\approx(83862,07173,-44104),\ \boldsymbol{T}_{3} ≈(−2.02757,−0.785264,1.15808).\displaystyle\approx(-02757,-785264,15808). (32)

At k=1.8k=1.8 there are two focus-focus-regular points, which we denote by

ff1≈(−0.578466,0.578466,1.8),ff2≈(−1.743013,1.743013,1.8),\mathrm{f{}f}_{1}\approx(-0.578466,0.578466,1.8),\ \mathrm{f{}f}_{2}\approx(-1.743013,1.743013,1.8),

while at k=0.3k=0.3 there are two focus-focus-regular points, which we denote by

ff3≈(1.793686,−1.164044,0.3),ff4≈(1.164044,−1.793686,0.3).\mathrm{f{}f}_{3}\approx(1.793686,-1.164044,0.3),\ \mathrm{f{}f}_{4}\approx(1.164044,-1.793686,0.3).

Then for each j=1,2,3,4j=1,2,3,4 we consider a closed path γj\gamma_{j} in the set ℛ\mathcal{R} of regular values of FF which is based at r0r_{0} and represents a homotopy class [γj][\gamma_{j}] in π1​(ℛ,r0)\pi_{1}(\mathcal{R},r_{0}). Specifically, the path γj\gamma_{j} starts at r0r_{0}, moves along a straight line to the regular value rj=ffj+(0.5,0,0)r_{j}=\mathrm{f{}f}_{j}+(0.5,0,0), circles the focus-focus point ffj\mathrm{f{}f}_{j} along the circle CjC_{j} parameterized by ffj+0.5​(cos⁡(2​π​s),sin⁡(2​π​s),0)\mathrm{f{}f}_{j}+0.5(\cos(2\pi s),\sin(2\pi s),0) with s∈[0,1]s\in[0,1], and then moves from rjr_{j} back to r0r_{0} along a straight line.

For each closed path γj\gamma_{j}, j=1,2,3,4j=1,2,3,4, we continue the period vectors 𝑻2\boldsymbol{T}_{2}, 𝑻3\boldsymbol{T}_{3} along the path, and when we arrive back at r0r_{0} we obtain the period vectors 𝑻2(j)\boldsymbol{T}^{(j)}_{2}, 𝑻3(j)\boldsymbol{T}^{(j)}_{3}. We do not numerically continue 𝑻1\boldsymbol{T}_{1} since it is constant. The Hamiltonian monodromy matrix along the path γj\gamma_{j} is given by

M​(γj)=ℬ​(𝑻1,𝑻2(j),𝑻3(j))​ℬ​(𝑻1,𝑻2,𝑻3)−1,M(\gamma_{j})=\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{(j)},\boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{(j)})\,\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2},\boldsymbol{T}_{3})^{-1},

where ℬ​(𝑻1,𝑻2,𝑻3)\mathcal{B}(\boldsymbol{T}_{1},\boldsymbol{T}_{2},\boldsymbol{T}_{3}) is the 3×33\times 3 matrix with rows given by the (row) vectors 𝑻1\boldsymbol{T}_{1}, 𝑻2\boldsymbol{T}_{2}, and 𝑻3\boldsymbol{T}_{3}.

The numerical computations are approximate and they produce matrices which are close to matrices in SL⁡(3,𝐙)\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}). In each case, we consider the nearest SL⁡(3,𝐙)\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) matrix as the accurate expression for M​(γj)M(\gamma_{j}).

Applying this procedure for the path γ1\gamma_{1} gives

𝑻2(1)≈(1.83862,2.07173,−1.44104),𝑻3(1)≈(−3.86619,−2.85699,2.59913).\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{(1)}\approx(1.83862,2.07173,-1.44104),\ \boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{(1)}\approx(-3.86619,-2.85699,2.59913).

The corresponding approximate monodromy matrix is computed as M~​(γ1)=M​(γ1)+O​(10−7)\widetilde{M}(\gamma_{1})=M(\gamma_{1})+O(10^{-7}), with exact monodromy matrix

M​(γ1)=[1000100−11].M(\gamma_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&-1&1\\ \end{bmatrix}.
Remark 21.

We report the result of the numerical computation of the monodromy matrix in the form M​(γ)+O​(ϵ)M(\gamma)+O(\epsilon), where M​(γ)∈SL⁡(3,𝐙)M(\gamma)\in\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) and ϵ≪1\epsilon\ll 1. This may obscure that the main problem in the computation is not the small numerical error in the approximation of the period vectors but that during continuation the Newton-Raphson method might converge to a different period vector. If this happens, the produced approximate monodromy matrix will still have the form M​(γ)+O​(ϵ)M(\gamma)+O(\epsilon), where M​(γ)∈SL⁡(3,𝐙)M(\gamma)\in\operatorname{SL}(3,\mathbf{Z}) and ϵ≪1\epsilon\ll 1, but M​(γ)M(\gamma) will be incorrect. We control for this error by checking during continuation the size of the change of the period vector.

Similarly, for the path γ2\gamma_{2} we compute

𝑻2(2)≈(1.64967,3.35819,−8.00719),𝑻3(2)≈(−1.83862,−2.07173,7.72423).\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{(2)}\approx(1.64967,3.35819,-8.00719),\ \boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{(2)}\approx(-1.83862,-2.07173,7.72423).

The corresponding approximate monodromy matrix is computed as M~​(γ2)=M​(γ2)+O​(10−6)\widetilde{M}(\gamma_{2})=M(\gamma_{2})+O(10^{-6}), with exact monodromy matrix

M​(γ2)=[100−1211−10].M(\gamma_{2})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ -1&2&1\\ 1&-1&0\\ \end{bmatrix}.

Then, for the path γ3\gamma_{3} we compute

𝑻2(3)≈(−0.188951,1.28646,−6.56615),𝑻3(3)≈(−2.02757,−0.785264,1.15808).\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{(3)}\approx(-0.188951,1.28646,-6.56615),\ \boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{(3)}\approx(-2.02757,-0.785264,1.15808).

The corresponding approximate monodromy matrix is computed as M~​(γ3)=M​(γ3)+O​(10−6)\widetilde{M}(\gamma_{3})=M(\gamma_{3})+O(10^{-6}), with exact monodromy matrix

M​(γ3)=[100−111001].M(\gamma_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ -1&1&1\\ 0&0&1\\ \end{bmatrix}.

Finally, for the path γ4\gamma_{4} we compute

𝑻2(4)≈(1.64967,3.35819,−8.00719),𝑻3(4)≈(−1.83862,−2.07173,7.72423).\boldsymbol{T}_{2}^{(4)}\approx(1.64967,3.35819,-8.00719),\ \boldsymbol{T}_{3}^{(4)}\approx(-1.83862,-2.07173,7.72423).

The corresponding approximate monodromy matrix is computed as M~​(γ4)=M​(γ4)+O​(10−6)\widetilde{M}(\gamma_{4})=M(\gamma_{4})+O(10^{-6}), with exact monodromy matrix

M​(γ4)=[100−1211−10].M(\gamma_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ -1&2&1\\ 1&-1&0\\ \end{bmatrix}.

The monodromy matrices reported in Section 5.2 are the same as the matrices reported here up to a change of basis. Specifically, consider the change of basis given by the matrix

A=[1001−1−1010].A=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 1&-1&-1\\ 0&1&0\end{bmatrix}.

Then, redefining M​(γj)M(\gamma_{j}) to be A​M​(γj)​A−1AM(\gamma_{j})A^{-1} we compute the reported

M​(γ1)=[100011001],M​(γ2)=M​(γ4)=[1000100−11],M​(γ3)=[1000210−10].\displaystyle M(\gamma_{1})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&1\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix},\ M(\gamma_{2})=M(\gamma_{4})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&1&0\\ 0&-1&1\end{bmatrix},\ M(\gamma_{3})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\ 0&2&1\\ 0&-1&0\end{bmatrix}.

Appendix D Spectral Lax pair

In this section, we briefly describe the spectral Lax pair formalism associated with the two-spin TC, see [7, 5]. We introduce the Lax matrices ℒ\mathcal{L} and ℳ\mathcal{M} depending on the spectral parameter λ∈𝐂\lambda\in\mathbf{C} given by

ℒ​(λ)=1g2​(λ−δ12)​(λ−δ22)​((2​λ−ω)​σz+i​2​g​(z¯​σ+−z​σ−))+(λ−δ22)​𝒖⋅𝝈+(λ−δ12)​𝒗⋅𝝈,\mathcal{L}(\lambda)=\frac{1}{g^{2}}\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)\bigl((2\lambda-\omega)\sigma_{z}+i\sqrt{2}g(\bar{z}\sigma_{+}-z\sigma_{-})\bigr)+\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)\boldsymbol{u}\cdot\boldsymbol{\sigma}+\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)\boldsymbol{v}\cdot\boldsymbol{\sigma},

and

ℳ​(λ)=−i​λ​σz+g2​(z¯​σ+−z​σ−),\mathcal{M}(\lambda)=-i\lambda\sigma_{z}+\frac{g}{\sqrt{2}}(\bar{z}\sigma_{+}-z\sigma_{-}),

where 𝒖=(u1,u2,u3)\boldsymbol{u}=(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3}), 𝒗=(v1,v2,v3)\boldsymbol{v}=(v_{1},v_{2},v_{3}) and 𝝈\boldsymbol{\sigma} is the vector of Pauli matrices (σx,σy,σz)(\sigma_{x},\sigma_{y},\sigma_{z}) with

σx=[0110],σy=[0−ii0],σz=[100−1],σ+=12​(σx+i​σy),σ−=12​(σx−i​σy).\sigma_{x}=\begin{bmatrix}0&1\\ 1&0\end{bmatrix},~\sigma_{y}=\begin{bmatrix}0&-i\\ i&0\end{bmatrix},~\sigma_{z}=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&-1\end{bmatrix},~\sigma_{+}=\frac{1}{2}(\sigma_{x}+i\sigma_{y}),~\sigma_{-}=\frac{1}{2}(\sigma_{x}-i\sigma_{y}).

It can be shown that the matrices ℒ\mathcal{L} and ℳ\mathcal{M} satisfy the Lax equation ℒ˙​(λ)=[ℳ​(λ),ℒ​(λ)]\dot{\mathcal{L}}(\lambda)=[\mathcal{M}(\lambda),\mathcal{L}(\lambda)] for any value of the complex parameter λ\lambda. Moreover, this equation is equivalent to the system’s dynamical equations.

The characteristic polynomial of ℒ​(λ)\mathcal{L}(\lambda) does not depend on time and defines the spectral curve given by the equation det(ℒ​(λ)−μ​I)=0\det(\mathcal{L}(\lambda)-\mu I)=0. For the two-spin TC, we obtain

μ2=Q6​(λ)\mu^{2}=Q_{6}(\lambda)

where Q6Q_{6} is a polynomial of degree 66 in λ\lambda that can be expressed as

Q6​(λ)\displaystyle Q_{6}(\lambda) =[(2​λ−ω)2g4+4​Kg2]​(λ−δ12)2​(λ−δ22)2+2g2​(λ−δ12)​(λ−δ22)​[H1​(λ−δ22)+H2​(λ−δ12)]\displaystyle=\Bigl[\frac{(2\lambda-\omega)^{2}}{g^{4}}+\frac{4K}{g^{2}}\Bigr]\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)^{2}\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)^{2}+\frac{2}{g^{2}}\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl[H_{1}\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)+H_{2}\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)\Bigr]
+(λ−δ12)2+(λ−δ22)2.\displaystyle\qquad+\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{1}}{2}\Bigr)^{2}+\Bigl(\lambda-\frac{\delta_{2}}{2}\Bigr)^{2}.

Note that all the coefficients of Q6​(λ)Q_{6}(\lambda) are constants of motion.

The central critical value of the STC system occurs when the polynomial Q6Q_{6} has two complex conjugate triple roots. In this case, the polynomial Q6Q_{6} can be expressed as

Q6​(λ)=4g4​(λ2+a1​λ+a0)3,Q_{6}(\lambda)=\frac{4}{g^{4}}(\lambda^{2}+a_{1}\lambda+a_{0})^{3},

where a0a_{0} and a1a_{1} are two real parameters such that a12−4​a0<0a_{1}^{2}-4a_{0}<0. Identifying the coefficients of the two expressions of Q6Q_{6} leads to six equations, from which the resonance condition 2​ω=δ1+δ22\omega=\delta_{1}+\delta_{2}, with δ1≠δ2\delta_{1}\neq\delta_{2}, is obtained.

In the STC system, we consider the parameters δ1=12\delta_{1}=\frac{1}{2}, δ2=32\delta_{2}=\frac{3}{2}, ω=1\omega=1 and g=1g=1. We deduce that a1=−1a_{1}=-1, a0=(4⋅22/3+3)/16a_{0}=(4\cdot 2^{2/3}+3)/16 and that the central critical value corresponds to the values H1=2−3⋅2−2/3H_{1}=2-3\cdot 2^{-2/3}, H2=−2+3⋅2−2/3H_{2}=-2+3\cdot 2^{-2/3}, K=(12⋅22/3−1)/16K=(12\cdot 2^{2/3}-1)/16.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
